- The curve shows that as the training set size grows, the accuracy increases.

In this graph, we reach 95% accuracy, and it looks like the curve might continue to increase with more data.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
### Information Gain and Entropy

- Counting the impurity of each attribute just tells us a rough importance.
- To have a more precise measure, we should employ an **Information gain**.
- Information gain is defined in terms of **Entropy** (Shannon and Weaver, 1949), to define a precise measure of an attribute's importance.
- Entropy is an uncertainty of a random variable.

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Intuition

- If a coin is fair, its uncertainty is very high (entropy = 1)
- If a coin always comes up heads, it has no uncertainty (entropy = 0)

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Entropy Formula

If a training set contains p positive examples and n negative examples:

```
Entropy: I(p/(p+n), n/(p+n)) = -(p/(p+n))·log₂(p/(p+n)) - (n/(p+n))·log₂(n/(p+n))
```

- We can check that the entropy of a fair coin flip is indeed 1:

```
I(1/2, 1/2) = -½·log₂(½) - ½·log₂(½) = 1
```

- If the coin gives 99% heads, we get:

```
I(99/100, 1/100) = -(99/100)·log₂(99/100) - (1/100)·log₂(1/100) = 0.08
```

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Remainder and Information Gain

- We can measure how much the entropy (uncertainty) remaining after testing the attribute A by:

```
Remainder(A) = Σᵥᵢ₌₁ ((pᵢ + nᵢ)/(p + n)) · I(pᵢ/(pᵢ + nᵢ), nᵢ/(pᵢ + nᵢ))
```

- The information gain from the attribute A is defined as:

```
Gain(A) = I(p/(p+n), n/(p+n)) - Remainder(A)
```

- The more value of the information gain of the attribute A, the more important it is.

Splitting on Attribute A:

| Branch | Positives | Negatives |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| 1      | +++       |           |
| 2      |           | ----      |
| 3      | ++++      |           |

The less the remainder, the more important the attribute A.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Example Calculation: Patrons vs. Type

- Now, let us calculate the information gain of both Patrons and Type.

```
Gain(Type) = I(6/12, 6/12) - [2/12 · I(1/2, 1/2) + 2/12 · I(1/2, 1/2) + 4/12 · I(2/4, 2/4) + 4/12 · I(2/4, 2/4)]
           = 0
```

```
Gain(Patrons) = I(6/12, 6/12) - [2/12 · I(0, 1) + 4/12 · I(1, 0) + 6/12 · I(2/6, 4/6)]
              = 1 - [0 + 0 + 0.4594]
              = 0.5406
```

Therefore, Patrons is more important than Type.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.section: Applying ID3 to a Regression Problem (Regression Tree)
chunks.content:
### Applying ID3 to a Regression Problem (Regression Tree)

- Regression is a problem of using the input values to predict continuous valued output (instead of a discrete output like a classification problem).

Example decision rule:
- Attribute A < 5.5 → Average = 1.3
- Attribute A > 11 → Average = 5.8
- Otherwise → Average = 3.1

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Finding the Root Split

- Just like for Classification Trees, the first thing we do for a Regression Tree is decide what goes in the Root.

First, find the average of the first 2 points and use it to split all data points.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Step-by-Step Threshold Search

**Threshold 1.5 (A < 1.5):**
- Left (1 point): Average = 1.0
- Right (14 points): Average = 3.9
- The dashed lines represent errors between individual points on the right and the average 3.9.
- Calculate the sum-squared error of all data points. Let's call it E1.

**Threshold 2.5 (A < 2.5):**
- Shift the threshold to the right (avg. of 2nd and 3rd points)
- Left (2 points): Average = 1.1
- Right (13 points): Average = 4.1
- Calculate the sum-squared error. Let's call it E2.

**Threshold 3.5 (A < 3.5):**
- Left (3 points): Average = 1.3
- Right (12 points): Average = 4.5
- Calculate the sum-squared error. Let's call it E3.

**…continue shifting until the last pair…**

**Threshold 14.5 (A < 14.5):**
- Left: Average = 3.0
- Right: Average = 4.2
- Calculate the sum-squared error. Let's call it E14.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Selecting the Best Threshold

Plot of Sum-squared error (SSE) across thresholds E1 … E14:

The best threshold is the one with the lowest SSE (e.g., threshold 5.5 → A < 5.5, Avg = 1.3).

- Discard all points on the left of the threshold.
- Repeat the process to find the next threshold.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
## Random Forest Algorithm

Two major problems of ID3 (Decision tree algorithm):
1. It is easily overfitted to the training data.
2. It is sensitive to the small change in the training data.

Solution: **Multiple decision trees.**

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 27](documents/ai-lec08-learning-from-examples-20260630.pdf#page=27) — Question: Why are there many different decision trees for the same training set?

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 28](documents/ai-lec08-learning-from-examples-20260630.pdf#page=28) — Answer: Each Decision Tree model in a Random Forest algorithm receives a different subset of training data (bootstrap or sampling).

Training data → Bootstrap/Sampling data

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 29](documents/ai-lec08-learning-from-examples-20260630.pdf#page=29) — Prediction on an unseen data point. Three decision trees vote: Yes, Yes, No → Majority vote = Yes.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.section: Advantages of the Random Forest Algorithm
chunks.content:
### Advantages of the Random Forest Algorithm

- Robust and less sensitive to small changes to our data. Since it aggregates the predictions of all decision tree models.
- Similar to the decision tree algorithm, Random Forest can be applied to classification and regression problems.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
## K Nearest Neighbors Algorithm (KNN)

- K nearest neighbors is a nonparametric algorithm that classifies unseen data based on a distance function.
- An example is classified by a majority vote of its K neighbors.

Applicable to both continuous and discrete features across multiple dimensions.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
### Example: Credit Default

- Consider the following data concerning credit default. Age and Loan are two numerical attributes and Default is the target class.
- Given an unseen example (Age=48 and Loan=$142,000):

The shortest Euclidean distance (K=1):

```
Distance = √((48 - 33)² + (142000 - 150000)²) = 8,000 → Default = Y
```

- With K=3, there are two Default=Y and one Default=N closest neighbors. The prediction for the unknown case is again Default=Y.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
### Standardized Distance

- One major drawback in calculating distance measures directly from the training set is in the case where variables have different measurement scales or there is a mixture of numerical and categorical variables.
- For example, if one variable is based on annual income in dollars, and the other is based on age in years then income will have a much higher influence on the distance calculated.
- One solution is to standardize the training set.
- Using the standardized distance on the same training set, the unknown example returned a different neighbor.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
## Evaluating the Hypothesis

- We can simply use the error rate to measure the hypothesis.
  - Error rate = Number of examples which are wrong predicted / Total number of examples
- However, a hypothesis h has a low error rate on the training set does not mean that it will generalize well.
- To get a more accurate evaluation, we need to test the hypothesis on an unseen set (test set) of examples.

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
### Holdout Cross-Validation

(If we have a large amount of dataset)

Randomly split the available data into a training set (e.g. 75%) and a test set (e.g. 25%).

chunks.title: Ai Lec08 Learning From Examples 20260630
chunks.content:
### K-Fold Cross-Validation

(If we have a limited amount of dataset)

- Split the training data into k equal subsets.
- Then perform k rounds of learning. On each round, 1/k of the data is held out as a validation set and the remaining examples are used as training data.
- The average error rate of the k rounds is finally calculated. Popular values for k are 5 and 10. The extreme is k=n (the total number of examples), also known as leave-one-out cross-validation or LOOCV.

---

> 📄 See [PDF pages 36–49](documents/ai-lec08-learning-from-examples-20260630.pdf#page=36) — K-Fold Cross Validation process visualized step by step. Training + Validation sets (75%) are partitioned into K folds. Each fold is used once as a validation set while the remaining K-1 folds form the training set. After K rounds, the final metrics are aggregated:

```
Training Accuracy = (Σᵢ₌₁ᴷ Train_Acc_i) / K
Validation Accuracy = (Σᵢ₌₁ᴷ Valid_Acc_i) / K
```

The model is then evaluated on the held-out Test Set (25%) to obtain Test Accuracy.

Note: Percentage of test set can be varied, depends on how many total instances we have.

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.section: AI Lec09 — Perceptron and Artificial Neural Networks (2026)
chunks.content:
# AI Lec09 — Perceptron and Artificial Neural Networks (2026)

> 📄 [View original PDF](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf) — source of truth

Artificial Intelligence
Instructor: Kietikul Jearanaitanakij
Department of Computer Engineering
King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology Ladkrabang

---

**Lecture 9 — Perceptron and Artificial Neural Networks**

- Linear Classification With Perceptron
- Perceptron learning algorithm
- Multilayer neural networks
- Backpropagation for multilayer NN

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Perceptron

- Perceptron is a computation unit that imitates a biological neuron.
- Dendrite receives an input signal.
- Axon sends signals out to other neurons.

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 4](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=4) — Inside a Perceptron diagram

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Perceptron at an Early Stage

```mermaid
graph LR
    x1["x1"] --> w1["w1"] --> Yi["Yi = Neuron_i"]
    x2["x2"] --> w2["w2"] --> Yi
    x3["x3"] --> w3["w3"] --> Yi
```

Perceptron consists of a single neuron with adjustable weights and a hard limit activation function.

Frank Rosenblatt invented perceptron in 1957.

There are many kinds of activation functions.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Activation Functions

| Name | Equation | Plot |
|------|----------|------|
| Identity (Linear) | — | — |
| Binary step | — | — |
| Sigmoid (Logistic) | — | — |
| TanH (Hyperbolic Tan) | — | — |
| Softmax | For classification problems. | — |

> 📄 See [PDF page 6](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=6) for activation function plots

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Linear Separability

Data is linearly separable if there is a hyperplane that separates their instances into 2 classes.

In general, the hyperplane is defined by the linearly separable function:

```
x1·w1 + x2·w2 - θ = 0
```

In general: Σ(i=1 to n) xᵢwᵢ − θ = 0

The threshold θ is used to shift the decision boundary up and down (vertical intercept).

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Perceptron Learning

- The perceptron learns its classification by making small adjustments in the weights to reduce the error between the actual and target outputs of the perceptron.

```
e(p) = Yd(p) − Y(p)
```

Where:
- p is the training pattern (example)
- Yd(p) is the target output of pattern p
- Y(p) is the actual output
- e(p) is the error between Yd(p) and Y(p)

- It uses e(p) to update weights of the next iteration:

```
wᵢ(p + 1) = wᵢ(p) + α · xᵢ(p) · e(p)
```

where α is the learning rate (0~1).

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Perceptron Learning Algorithm

**Step 1: Initialization**

Set initial weights w₁, w₂, …, wₙ and thresholds (θ) to small random numbers in the range [-0.5, +0.5]. p = 1 (the first pattern).

**Step 2: Activation**

```
Y(p) = step( Σ(i=1 to n) xᵢ(p) · wᵢ(p) − θ )
```

where n is the number of perceptron inputs. (Step function)

**Step 3: Weight training by using gradient descent**

```
wᵢ(p + 1) = wᵢ(p) + Δwᵢ(p)
Δwᵢ(p) = α · xᵢ(p) · e(p)
```

where `e(p) = Yd(p) − Y(p)`

---

**Step 4: Iteration**

Increase p by one, go back to step 2 until each pattern is trained.

**Step 5:**

If the perceptron doesn't converge, p = 1 and repeat steps 2–4.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.section: Example: Train a Perceptron on AND Operator

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Problem of a Perceptron

- Perceptron can learn only simple linear separable problems, e.g., AND, OR. It failed on XOR problem.
- A single perceptron can classify only linear separable problems, regardless of whether we use hard-limit or soft-limit activation functions.
- Moreover, increasing the number of perceptrons in the same layer doesn't help.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Multilayer Neural Networks

- Input layer accepts input signals and distributes these signals to all neurons in the next hidden layer.
- Hidden layers detect the features of the input patterns by adjusting the weights of the neurons.
- Output layer accepts signals from the hidden layer and predicts the output class.
- Multilayer neural networks can solve the non-linearly separable problem.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Nonlinearly Separable Problem

| AND | OR | XOR |
|-----|----|-----|
| Linearly separable | Linearly separable | **Non-linearly separable** |

> 📄 See [PDF page 14](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=14) for decision boundary diagrams

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Choosing Hidden Layers and Neurons

- The criteria for choosing the number of hidden layers and neurons depends on the problem's complexity.
- Good practice is to start with a low number of them and gradually increase until the network can learn the patterns.
- Most practical applications use a three-layer neural network (1-1-1: input-hidden-output).
- The most popular learning algorithm is **Backpropagation**.

Each neuron determines its output Y as:

```
X = Σ(i=1 to n) xᵢwᵢ − θ
y = 1 / (1 + e⁻ˣ)   ; 0 < y < 1  (Sigmoid)
```

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 16](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=16) — Forward computation and backpropagation network diagram

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Backpropagation for Multilayer NN

- To propagate error signals, we start at the output layer and work backward to the hidden layer.
- The error signal at the output of neuron k at iteration p is defined by:

```
eₖ(p) = yₐₖ(p) − yₖ(p)
```

Where `yₐₖ(p)` is the desired output of neuron k at iteration p.

---

**Weight update (output layer):**

```
wⱼₖ(p + 1) = wⱼₖ(p) + Δwⱼₖ(p)
Δwⱼₖ(p) = α · yⱼ(p) · δₖ(p)
```

**Error gradient at neuron k:**

```
δₖ(p) = ∂yₖ(p)/∂Xₖ(p) · eₖ(p)
```

For a sigmoid activation function (yₖ = 1/(1 + e⁻ˣᵏ)):

```
∂yₖ(p)/∂Xₖ(p) = e⁻ˣᵏ⁽ᵖ⁾ / (1 + e⁻ˣᵏ⁽ᵖ⁾)²
               = yₖ(p) · (1 − yₖ(p))
```

---

**Weight update (hidden layer):**

```
wᵢⱼ(p + 1) = wᵢⱼ(p) + Δwᵢⱼ(p)
Δwᵢⱼ(p) = α · xᵢ(p) · δⱼ(p)
```

```
δⱼ(p) = yⱼ(p) · (1 − yⱼ(p)) · Σ(q=1 to l) [δₖq(p) · wⱼₖq(p)]
```

Where l is the number of neurons in the output layer.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Complete Training Algorithm

**Step 1: Weights Initialization**

Set all weights and thresholds (θ) to random numbers uniformly distributed inside a small range, e.g., (-0.5, +0.5).

**Step 2: Activation (Feedforward computation)**

Calculate the actual outputs of neurons in the **hidden layer**:

```
yⱼ(p) = sigmoid( Σ(i=1 to n) xᵢ(p) · wᵢⱼ(p) − θⱼ )
```

Where n is the number of inputs of neuron j in the hidden layer.

---

Calculate the actual outputs of neurons in the **output layer**:

```
yₖ(p) = sigmoid( Σ(j=1 to m) xⱼ(p) · wⱼₖ(p) − θₖ )
```

Where m is the number of inputs of neuron k in the output layer.

---

**Step 3: Weight training (Backpropagation)**

Calculate the error gradient of neurons in the **hidden-output layer**:

```
eₖ(p) = yₐₖ(p) − yₖ(p)
δₖ(p) = yₖ(p) · (1 − yₖ(p)) · eₖ(p)
Δwⱼₖ(p) = α · yⱼ(p) · δₖ(p)
wⱼₖ(p + 1) = wⱼₖ(p) + Δwⱼₖ(p)
```

Calculate the error gradient of neurons in the **input-hidden layer**:

```
δⱼ(p) = yⱼ(p) · (1 − yⱼ(p)) · Σ(k=1 to l) δₖ(p) · wⱼₖ(p)
Δwᵢⱼ(p) = α · xᵢ(p) · δⱼ(p)
wᵢⱼ(p + 1) = wᵢⱼ(p) + Δwᵢⱼ(p)
```

---

**Step 4: Iterations**

- Take the next training pattern, p+1, and go back to step 2. Then repeat the process until the selected error criterion is satisfied.
- A simple stop criterion is when the sum-squared error (SSE) is less than a certain number, e.g., 0.1.

```
SSE = Σ(p=1 to #patterns) Σ(k=1 to #outputs) (yₐₖ(p) − yₖ(p))²
```

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
### Example: 3-Layer NN for XOR Problem

Recall that a single-layer perceptron cannot solve XOR problem.

> 📄 See [PDF page 24](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=24) — 3-layer XOR network architecture

> 📄 See [PDF page 25](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=25) — XOR network with initial weights

---

**All weights and thresholds are randomly initialized as follows:**

| Parameter | Initial Value |
|-----------|--------------|
| w₁₃ | 0.5 |
| w₁₄ | 0.9 |
| w₂₃ | 0.4 |
| w₂₄ | 1.0 |
| w₃₅ | -1.2 |
| w₄₅ | 1.1 |
| θ₃ | 0.8 |
| θ₄ | -0.1 |
| θ₅ | 0.3 |

---

**Forward computation for training pattern ⟨1, 1, 0⟩:**

```
y₃ = 1 / (1 + e^(−(1·0.5 + 1·0.4 − 1·0.8))) = 0.5250
y₄ = 1 / (1 + e^(−(1·0.9 + 1·1.0 + 1·0.1)))  = 0.8808
y₅ = 1 / (1 + e^(−(−0.525·1.2 + 0.8808·1.1 − 1·0.3))) = 0.5097

e = yₐ₅ − y₅ = 0 − 0.5097 = −0.5097

δ₅ = y₅ · (1 − y₅) · e
   = 0.5097 · (1 − 0.5097) · (−0.5097)
   = −0.1274
```

---

**Weight updates (α = 0.1):**

```
Δw₃₅ = α · y₃ · δ₅ = 0.1 · 0.5250 · (−0.1274) = −0.0067
Δw₄₅ = α · y₄ · δ₅ = 0.1 · 0.8808 · (−0.1274) = −0.0112
Δθ₅  = α · (−1) · δ₅ = 0.1 · (−1) · (−0.1274) = 0.0127
```

**Error gradients for hidden neurons:**

```
δ₃ = y₃ · (1 − y₃) · δ₅ · w₃₅ = 0.525 · (1 − 0.525) · (−0.1274) · (−1.2) = 0.0381
δ₄ = y₄ · (1 − y₄) · δ₅ · w₄₅ = 0.8808 · (1 − 0.8808) · (−0.1274) · 1.1 = −0.0147
```

---

**Updating input-hidden weights:**

chunks.title: Ai Lec09 Perceptron Neural Nets 20260630
chunks.content:
```
Δw₁₃ = α · x₁ · δ₃ = 0.1 · 1 · 0.0381 = 0.0038
Δw₂₃ = α · x₂ · δ₃ = 0.1 · 1 · 0.0381 = 0.0038
Δθ₃  = α · (−1) · δ₃ = 0.1 · (−1) · 0.0381 = −0.0038

Δw₁₄ = α · x₁ · δ₄ = 0.1 · 1 · (−0.0147) = −0.0015
Δw₂₄ = α · x₂ · δ₄ = 0.1 · 1 · (−0.0147) = −0.0015
Δθ₄  = α · (−1) · δ₄ = 0.1 · (−1) · (−0.0147) = 0.0015
```

---

**Final updated weights:**

```
w₁₃ = 0.5 + 0.0038 = 0.5038
w₁₄ = 0.9 − 0.0015 = 0.8985
w₂₃ = 0.4 + 0.0038 = 0.4038
w₂₄ = 1.0 − 0.0015 = 0.9985
w₃₅ = −1.2 − 0.0067 = −1.2067
w₄₅ = 1.1 − 0.0112 = 1.0888
θ₃  = 0.8 − 0.0038 = 0.7962
θ₄  = −0.1 + 0.0015 = −0.0985
θ₅  = 0.3 + 0.0127 = 0.3127
```

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 31](documents/ai-lec09-perceptron-neural-nets-20260630.pdf#page=31) — SSE vs. Epoch convergence graph

- Repeat the same computation for all training patterns (1 epoch)
- Repeat the process for another epoch until the sum of squared error (SSE) is less than a certain number, e.g. 0.001.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
# AI Lec10 — Genetic Algorithm (2026)

> 📄 [View original PDF](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf) — source of truth

Artificial Intelligence
Instructor: Kietikul Jearanaitanakij
Department of Computer Engineering
King Mongkut's Institute of Technology Ladkrabang

---

**Lecture 10 — Genetic Algorithm**

- Evolution
- Steps of Genetic Algorithm
- Roulette wheel selection
- Crossover operator
- Mutation operator
- GA case studies

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Genetic Algorithm (GA)

- In the early 1970s, John Holland introduced the concept of genetic algorithms.
- GA combines three concepts of evolution, natural selection, and genetics to search for the optimal solution.
- Optimization iteratively improves the quality of solutions until the optimal solution is found.
- We use the **fitness function** to measure the quality of a solution.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### GA Representation and Steps

- GA represents a solution as a **chromosome** consisting of some "genes".
- Each gene is represented by 0 or 1:

```
1 1 0 1 0 1 0 0 0 0 1 0 1
```

**Steps in GA:**

1. Create a population of chromosomes.
2. Evaluate their fitness.
3. Create a new population through genetic operations.
4. Repeat this process many times.

**Genetic operations:**

1. **Crossover** — exchanges parts of two single chromosomes.
2. **Cloning** — copies chromosomes.
3. **Mutation** — changes the gene value in some random locations of a chromosome.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Full Steps of Genetic Algorithm

**Step 1:**
- Encode a solution as a chromosome of a fixed length.
- Define population size N, crossover probability pc, and mutation probability pm.

**Step 2:**
Define a fitness function to measure the performance, or fitness, of an individual chromosome in the problem domain.

**Step 3:**
Randomly generate an initial population of chromosomes of size N: {x₁, x₂, …, xₙ}

---

**Step 4:**
Calculate the fitness of each chromosome:

```
f(x₁), f(x₂), …, f(xₙ)
```

**Step 5:**
Select a pair of chromosomes for mating from the current population.

**Step 6:**
Create a pair of offspring chromosomes by applying the genetic operators — crossover/cloning and mutation.

**Step 7:**
Place the created offspring chromosomes in the new population.

**Step 8:**
Repeat Step 5 until the size of the new chromosome population becomes equal to the size of the initial population, N.

---

**Step 9:**
Replace the initial (parent) chromosome population with the new (offspring) population.

**Step 10:**
Go to Step 4, and repeat the process until the termination criterion is satisfied.

- Each iteration is called a **generation**. A typical number of generations for a simple GA can range from 50 to over 500.
- The entire set of generations is called a **run**.
- GA returns the best chromosomes in the population as a final solution.

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 8](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=8) — The genetic algorithm cycle diagram

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.section: How to Select Chromosomes? — Roulette Wheel Selection
chunks.content:
### How to Select Chromosomes? — Roulette Wheel Selection

The most commonly used chromosome selection technique is the **roulette wheel selection**.

Example: 36 · 100 / (36 + 44 + 14 + 14 + 56 + 54) = 16.5%

| Chromosome | Fitness | Ratio |
|------------|---------|-------|
| X1 | 36 | 16.5% |
| X2 | 44 | 20.2% |
| X3 | 14 | 6.4% |
| X4 | 14 | 6.4% |
| X5 | 56 | 25.3% |
| X6 | 54 | 24.8% |

> 📄 See [PDF page 9](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=9) — Roulette wheel diagram

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Crossover / Cloning Operator

- IF random ≤ crossover probability pc, then do the **crossover**.
- The crossover operator randomly chooses a crossover point where two parent chromosomes "break".
- Exchanges the chromosome parts.
- As a result, two new offspring are created.
- Else do the chromosome **cloning**. The offspring are created as exact copies of their parents.

> 📄 See [PDF page 11](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=11) — Crossover and cloning diagram

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Mutation Operator

- Mutation represents a change in the gene.
- The mutation operator randomly flips a gene in a chromosome.
- Mutation can avoid a trap on a local optima.
- The mutation probability is quite small in nature and is kept low for GAs, typically in the range between 0.001 and 0.01.

IF random ≤ pm → flip the gene. IF random > pm → keep the gene.

> 📄 See [PDF page 13](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=13) — Mutation operator diagram

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Genetic Algorithms: Case Study 1

Find the maximum value of the function **f(x) = 15x − x²** where x varies between 0 and 15.

For simplicity, we assume that x takes only integer values and a chromosome is represented as four genes:

| Integer | Binary | Integer | Binary | Integer | Binary |
|---------|--------|---------|--------|---------|--------|
| 0 | 0000 | 6 | 0110 | 11 | 1011 |
| 1 | 0001 | 7 | 0111 | 12 | 1100 |
| 2 | 0010 | 8 | 1000 | 13 | 1101 |
| 3 | 0011 | 9 | 1001 | 14 | 1110 |
| 4 | 0100 | 10 | 1010 | 15 | 1111 |
| 5 | 0101 | | | | |

---

**Parameters:**

- The size of the chromosome population N is 6.
- The crossover probability pc equals 0.7.
- The mutation probability pm equals 0.001.
- The fitness function: **f(x) = 15x − x²**

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Initial Population (N = 6)

| Chromosome Label | Chromosome String | Decoded Integer | Fitness | Fitness Ratio |
|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|---------|---------------|
| X1 | 1 1 0 0 | 12 | 36 | 16.5% |
| X2 | 0 1 0 0 | 4 | 44 | 20.2% |
| X3 | 0 0 0 1 | 1 | 14 | 6.4% |
| X4 | 1 1 1 0 | 14 | 14 | 6.4% |
| X5 | 0 1 1 1 | 7 | 56 | 25.7% |
| X6 | 1 0 0 1 | 9 | 54 | 24.8% |

> 📄 See [PDF page 16](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=16) for chromosome initial and final location graphs

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Genetic Algorithms: Case Study 2

Find the maximum of the function of two variables:

```
f(x, y) = (1 − x)² · e^(−x² − (y+1)²) − (x − x³ − y³) · e^(−x² − y²)
```

where parameters x and y vary between -3 and 3.

The first step is to represent the problem variables as a chromosome — parameters x and y as a concatenated binary string:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph x["x (8 bits)"]
        direction LR
        b1["1"] --- b2["0"] --- b3["0"] --- b4["0"] --- b5["1"] --- b6["0"] --- b7["1"] --- b8["0"]
    end
    subgraph y["y (8 bits)"]
        direction LR
        c1["0"] --- c2["0"] --- c3["1"] --- c4["1"] --- c5["1"] --- c6["0"] --- c7["1"] --- c8["1"]
    end
```

---

**Encoding/Decoding:**

Extract two 8-bit strings from the chromosome:

- X = `10001010`(binary) = 138(decimal)
- Y = `00111011`(binary) = 59(decimal)

Now the range of integers that can be handled by 8-bits (0 to 2⁸ − 1) is mapped to the actual range of parameters x and y (-3 to 3):

```
Scale factor = 6 / (256 − 1) = 0.0235294

x = 0.0235294 · 138 − 3 = 0.2470588
y = 0.0235294 · 59 − 3  = −1.6117647
```

---

> 📄 See [PDF page 20](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=20) — Chromosome locations on the "peak" function surface

> 📄 See [PDF page 21](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=21) — Chromosome locations: first generation

> 📄 See [PDF page 22](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=22) — Chromosome locations: mid generations

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
> 📄 See [PDF page 23](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=23) — Chromosome locations: final generation

---

chunks.content:

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.section: Case Study 3: Scheduling of 7 Power Units in 4 Quarters
chunks.content:
### Case Study 3: Scheduling of 7 Power Units in 4 Quarters

**Problem constraints:**

- The maximum loads expected during four quarters are 80, 90, 65, and 70 MW.
- Maintenance of any unit starts at the beginning of an interval and finishes at the end of the same or adjacent interval.
- The net reserve of the power system must be greater or equal to zero at any interval.
- The optimum criterion is the maximum of the net reserve at any maintenance period.

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Chromosome Encoding for Scheduling

Each unit has a gene pool representing legal maintenance start intervals. The chromosome is a concatenation of all unit schedules.

> 📄 See [PDF page 29](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=29) — Legal unit gene pools and chromosome encoding

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Crossover and Mutation Operators

> 📄 See [PDF page 30](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=30) — Crossover operator for scheduling problem

> 📄 See [PDF page 31](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=31) — Mutation operator for scheduling problem

---

chunks.title: Ai Lec10 Genetic Algorithm 20260630
chunks.content:
### Performance Results

**Population of N = 20 chromosomes, pc = 0.7, pm = 0.001:**

**(a) 50 generations — Best schedule net reserves: 15, 35, 35, 25 MW**

> 📄 See [PDF page 32](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=32) — Performance graph and schedule: N=20, 50 generations

**(b) 100 generations — Best schedule net reserves: 40, 25, 20, 25 MW**

> 📄 See [PDF page 33](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=33) — Performance graph and schedule: N=20, 100 generations

---

**Population of N = 100 chromosomes, pc = 0.7:**

**(a) Mutation rate pm = 0.001 — Best schedule net reserves: 35, 25, 25, 25 MW**

> 📄 See [PDF page 34](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=34) — Performance graph and schedule: N=100, pm=0.001

**(b) Mutation rate pm = 0.01 — Best schedule net reserves: 25, 25, 30, 30 MW**

> 📄 See [PDF page 35](documents/ai-lec10-genetic-algorithm-20260630.pdf#page=35) — Performance graph and schedule: N=100, pm=0.01

chunks.content: ### 1.10 Essential situations or developments to be considered in program planning

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 1.10.1 Economic situation/development

Amid the COVID 19 pandemic situation, the transformation to the digital economy is still an
essential change for the long-term development of the country. The digital economy, the

economy based on digital computing technologies, has become more important over the
years. Many objectives in the draft of the Thirteen National Economic and Social
Development Plan demonstrate the necessities of the transformation to the digital economy at
different levels. As automation technology progresses, the demand for low-skilled workers
constantly declines. Businesses need high-skilled workers who are well equipped with broad
skills to keep up with the global trends such as data-driven decisions and machine
intelligence. The COVID 19 pandemic also accelerates the situation. Businesses need to
adopt online approaches when physical contacts are restricted. Technological disruption in
the global economy may cause some businesses to fade out if they are unable to adapt their
organizations for digital transformation.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 1.10.2 Social and cultural situation/development

Inequality is a multidimensional issue in Thai society. The draft of the Thirteen National
Economic and Social Development Plan also expresses the same concern over the solutions
to this chronic problem, yet unable to implement them properly due to the lack of democratic
stability and the low effectiveness of the government in economic development.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 1.11 Impacts from 1.10.1 and 1.10.2 on the development of the program and relevance to the institutional missions
chunks.content: ### 1.11 Impacts from 1.10.1 and 1.10.2 on the development of the program and relevance to the institutional missions

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 1.11.1 Impacts on the development of the program
chunks.content:
#### 1.11.1 Impacts on the development of the program

Core courses of this program cover the fundamentals of many areas in computer
engineering to develop computer personnel with broad skills. In order to respond to
the demand for automation, the program also includes artificial intelligence and
machine learning in its core courses.
The main objectives of this program do not address the inequality issue directly. The
program, however, plans to allocate a part of its budget for student scholarships.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 1.11.2 Impacts on the institutional missions

The education in this program aligns with the institutional missions for KMITL.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222

chunks.content:

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 2.1.1 Philosophy

Research and education in computer engineering are important parts of the foundation
for the progress of the nation.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 2.1.2 Significance

This program aims to develop graduate students in computer engineering, in accordance with
the national qualifications framework for higher education and national economic and social
development plan, to respond to the workforce demand from both public and private sectors.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 2.1.3 Objectives

##### 2.1.3.1 Program objectives

1. To develop computer personnel with T-shaped skills, which cover a broad set of
theoretical foundations in mathematics, computer science, and practical skills in
information technology of their interests.
2. To develop ethical and social-responsible computer personnel that recognize the impact
of computer engineering solutions to both locally and globally contexts.
3. To develop computer personnel who can communicate effectively and work
collaboratively with other professionals.
4. To stimulate research and innovative work from students and academic staff.
5. To promote entrepreneurship in young computer professionals and encourage them to
apply engineering solutions for business opportunities.
##### 2.1.3.2 Program learning outcomes (PLOs)

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
This program adopts the student’s expected learning outcomes recommended by ABET’s
Criteria for Accrediting Engineering Programs.
Upon graduation, the student is expected to possess the following abilities:
1. an ability to identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems by applying
principles of engineering, science, and mathematics
2. an ability to apply engineering design to produce solutions that meet specified needs with
consideration of public health, safety, and welfare, as well as global, cultural, social,
environmental, and economic factors
3. an ability to communicate effectively with a range of audiences
4. an ability to recognize ethical and professional responsibilities in engineering situations
and make informed judgments, which must consider the impact of engineering solutions
in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts
5. an ability to function effectively on a team whose members together provide leadership,
create a collaborative and inclusive environment, establish goals, plan tasks, and meet
objectives
6. an ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze, and interpret
data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions

7. an ability to acquire and apply new knowledge as needed, using appropriate learning
strategies
##### 2.1.3.3 Correlation between program objectives and PLOs

> 📄 See original PDF page 12 (page number 6) for the correlation matrix.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 2.2 Program revision and development plan

| # | Plan | Strategy | Evidence / Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Program and teaching management development plan to meet international standards | Adopt TABEE's accreditation standards; Adopt ABET's accreditation standards; Adopt outcome-based student evaluation | The program gets accredited by TABEE; The program gets accredited by ABET; Student's satisfaction toward the program's teaching management |
| 2 | Lecturer development plan | Encourage lecturers to attend teaching development training | Percentage of lecturers who attend teaching development training; Student evaluation of teaching |
| 3 | Student development plan | Promote student leadership and teamwork | Number of courses that incorporate group assignments and presentation |

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 2.3 Learning outcomes after the completion of each academic year
chunks.content:
### 2.3 Learning outcomes after the completion of each academic year

After the completion of each academic year, students will be able to:

**Year 1:**
- Identify, analyze, and solve simple problems in general physics
- Identify, analyze, and solve problems in basic calculus
- Identify, analyze, experiment, and solve basic electrical circuit problems
- Identify, analyze, experiment, and solve simple problems in digital logic design
- Design, develop, and test computer programs with both procedural programming and object-oriented programming concepts

**Year 2:**
- Identify, analyze, and solve simple problems in ordinary differential equations, linear algebra, discrete mathematics
- Apply probability theory and statistics for data analysis
- Recall and explain the fundamentals of computer organization and computer networks
- Employ database systems for information management as well as analyze and model the relationship of data entities
- Recognize and apply fundamental data structures and algorithms in computer programming to solve problems systematically
- Recognize and apply the best practices in software development systematically
- Recognize the importance of web technologies and develop web applications that conform to the industry standards

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 2.3 Learning outcomes after the completion of each academic year
chunks.content:
**Year 3:**
- Design and develop simple embedded systems using microcontroller
- Recognize the fundamentals of operating systems and apply multithreading in computer programming
- Recognize the fundamentals of information security and apply related technologies in practice
- Recognize the foundation of artificial intelligence and apply theory and related technologies to solve practical problems
- Work as a part of a team, both as a leader and a team member

**Year 4:**
- Identify real-world problems that can be solved with applications of computer engineering
- Apply theoretical knowledge in mathematics to formulate and solve problems in computer engineering
- Work in team to design, build, and test prototypes with real-world applications
- Plan and manage computer engineering projects with systematic approaches
- Communicate the ideas and body of work in computer engineering both verbally and written
- Recognize the importance of work ethics and social responsibility

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.1.1 Academic term

A semester system is used for this program. One academic year consists of two semesters. A
semester lasts 15 weeks.
Details of the academic system at KMITL can be found in the Regulation of King Mongkut’s
Institute of Technology Ladkrabang on Undergraduate Study B.E. 2564.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.1.2 Additional academic term

Summer semester may be offered as required.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.2.1 Academic calendar

Usual study is conducted during normal working hours on weekdays.

| Semester | Period |
|---|---|
| Semester 1 | August – December |
| Semester 2 | January – May |
| Summer | June – July |

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.2.2 Admission requirements

Applicants are expected to obtain high school diploma or the equivalency.
Applicants are expected to satisfy all the admission requirements specified in the Regulation
of King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology Ladkrabang on Undergraduate Study B.E. 2564.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.2.3 New students’ expected problems

Students may have insufficient knowledge of mathematics or skills in mathematical
problem solving.
Students may have difficulties in understanding lecture or in communicating with
colleagues or staff.
Students may be unfamiliar with college-level study. Particularly, they must be
responsible for their learning.
The new students who have moved from home to live in an accommodation close to
the university will typically have more freedom in living their lives and, consequently,
some of those students may have problems in managing their time and the balance
between study and other activities.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 3.2.4 Strategies to mitigate problems stated in 3.2.3
chunks.content:
#### 3.2.4 Strategies to mitigate problems stated in 3.2.3

Evaluate the applicants’ knowledge and skills of English language and mathematics.

Provide remedial English language courses for the new students who are weak in
English.
Organize an orientation session for the new students, including an introduction to
university study and advice on planning their study and future careers.
Organize a pre-sessional course to lay foundational skills and knowledge for studying
in the program and get them accustomed to university lecture.
Provide an academic advisor for each student who is responsible for monitoring the
student’s academic progress and provide consultations to the student.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 3.2.5 Five-year plan for student admission and graduation

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.2.7 Delivery mode

On-campus in-class education, supplemented by online learning activities, practical
laboratory, and tutorial sessions.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.2.8 Credit transfer system

Details of credit transfer can be found in the Regulation of King Mongkut’s Institute of
Technology Ladkrabang on Undergraduate Study B.E. 2564 (Appendix A) and the
Proclamation of King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology Ladkrabang on Cross-University
Registration (Appendix B).

| | `96` = General Education (International Program) |

| | `27` = Computer Engineering (International Program) |
| 5th | Course level |
| | `6` = Undergraduate |
| 6th | Suggested year of study |

| | `5` = Elective, 3rd year |

##### 3.3.1.5 Course Descriptions

See Appendix D.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 3.4 Field experience courses (internships or co-operative education)
chunks.content:
### 3.4 Field experience courses (internships or co-operative education)

All students in this program are required to take an internship at least once after their 3rd year
of study. Co-operative education is also available for any student who wants to gain more
field experience.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.4.1 Standard learning outcomes

By the end of internship, students will be able to:
- Know the organizational structure and the overall goals and operations of the
company/organization and the functions of the department/division/working group in
which the students did their internships
- Have a clear understanding of the assigned tasks and a clear plan to perform those
tasks
- Complete the assigned tasks with the time and resources given
- Be involved in software development activities and apply the knowledge and skills
learned from the class on their work
- Communicate with all groups of people that the student contacted with as part of their
internship
- Work effectively under the supervision of a supervisor and as part of a team
- Acquire new technical knowledge and skills that are useful in completing the assigned
tasks

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.4.2 Time frame

- Summer semester between the 3rd year and 4th year of study for students who choose
capstone design project
- The first semester of 4th year of study for students who choose co-operative education
or overseas training

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 3.5 Requirements on project and research work
chunks.content:
### 3.5 Requirements on project and research work

The program offers capstone design project 1 and 2, which allow the students to study, apply
theoretical knowledge, design solutions, and implement prototype to problems in computer
engineering.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.5.1 Standard learning outcomes

By the end of capstone design project 1 and 2, students will be able to:
- Analyze and propose solutions to problems in computer engineering
- Design and develop prototypes that satisfy project requirements
- Utilize modern computer engineering tools
- Work in groups, as either leaders or team members
- Communicate effectively in both speech and writing

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.5.3 Credits

- Capstone Design Project 1: 3 (0-9-5)
- Capstone Design Project 2: 3 (0-9-5)

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.5.4 Preparation

1. Let students form group of 2-3 people. With consult from supervisors, each group
chooses a topic for a capstone project.
2. Supervisors advise their students on how to conduct research and assessments.
3. Supervisors schedule supervision hour.
4. Each group presents the project progress to the project assessment committee for
suggestion and assessment.
5. Students submit their completed theses for evaluation.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 3.5.5 Evaluation

1. From discussion among students and supervisors, each group sets the project’s goals
and evaluation methods that align with course objectives.
2. Each group presents the project result to the evaluation committee.
3. The project evaluation committee concludes the result for each group.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 4.1 Developmental characteristics of students

| Characteristic | Strategy or Student Activities |
|---|---|
| Integrity | Include academic honesty statements in the examinations; Incorporate into the program a compulsory course that covers topics related to ethics and law |
| Intellectual curiosity | Encourage the students to ask questions in-class; Adopt active learning strategies with various activities, such as discussion and problem-solving; Adopt project-based learning that allows the students to choose their own topics of interest for their assignments |
| Conscientiousness | Incorporate into many courses the laboratories and/or practices from the first year of study; Incorporate project courses in the last year of study; Allow the students to choose cooperative education |

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 4.2 Development of the learning outcomes in each domain of learning
chunks.content:
### 4.2 Development of the learning outcomes in each domain of learning

Learning outcomes according to Thai Qualifications Framework for Higher Education for
Computer B.E. 2552

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 4.2.1 Morality and ethics

##### 4.2.1.1 Learning outcomes

1. Perceive the importance of values, morals, ethics, self-sacrifice, and honesty
2. Have discipline, punctuality, and self and social responsibility
3. Have a team-working skill, be a good leader and a good team member, and be able to
resolve conflicts and prioritize the problems arising in the team
4. Respect other people’s rights, listen to others’ opinions, and respect the value and the
honor of being human
5. Respect the rules and the regulations of the institution and the society
6. Be able to evaluate the effects of computer usage on individuals, organizations, and
societies
7. Possess academic and professional ethics
##### 4.2.1.2 Teaching strategies

- Emphasize disciplines, punctuality, and submission of assignments on time
- Encourage (Provide opportunities for) the students to participate in activities which
benefit the society and build up good moral and ethical attitudes
- Encourage proper classroom etiquettes and dress codes
- Praise and/or reward good conduct
- Discourage dishonesty and misconduct, including plagiarism, by introducing punitive
measures and warning the students the consequences of their misconduct
- Set a good moral and ethical example for the students

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
- Invite a speaker to lecture about moral and ethics
- Provide opportunities for group discussions
- Encourage the students to participate in group discussions and make them learn to be
open mind and listen to other people’s opinions
- Discuss the impact of the subject or the course of study on people, organizations, and
societies
- Teach the students about the professional ethics and related regulations, and stress the
consequences of misconduct and negligence in profession on organizations, societies,
and themselves
- Provide opportunities for team working
- Teach the students about intellectual property and be aware and realize the
seriousness of plagiarism
##### 4.2.1.3 Assessment strategies

- Assign group homework or projects to the students and evaluate their team-working
skill
- Observe and evaluate class attendance and punctuality by regularly performing
attendance checks
- Observe and evaluate classroom etiquettes and dress codes
- Be vigilant of plagiarisms in the students’ submitted work and promptly offer
warnings and/or enforce punitive measures
- Observe and evaluate the students’ responsibility and punctuality in submitting
homework assignments

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 4.2.2 Knowledge

##### 4.2.2.1 Learning outcomes

1. Have acquired knowledge and understandings of important principles and theories of
the subjects in the field of study
2. Be able to analyze problems, understand and explain computing requirements, as well
as be able to apply knowledge, skills, and the usage of suitable tools for solving
problems
3. Be able to analyze, design, deploy, improve, and/or evaluate computing systems and
their components in accordance with the requirements
4. Be able to keep themselves up to date with academic progress and computing
evolutions, as well as their applications
5. Know, understand, and be interested in acquiring new knowledge and developing
their skills in computing continuously
6. Have broad knowledge in the field of study, to anticipate the changes and understand
the effects of new related technology
7. Have experiences in designing and developing software of real use
8. Be able to integrate the knowledge in their field of study with the knowledge in other
fields


##### 4.2.2.2 Teaching strategies

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
- Plan the syllabus to cover the necessary topics in the course within the given
timeframe
- Deliver the lecture with the aid of presentation slides and employ additional learning
resources, e.g., computer programs, audio/video materials, models, and prototypes
- Suggest additional reading materials
- Include practical training or exercises
- Assign homework or projects to provide the opportunities for the students to train
their skills related to the course (such as programming skills), as well as their
problem-solving skills and self-study skills
- Invite guest speakers from the industry
- Adopt a problem-based learning strategy to provide an opportunity for the students to
develop their problem-solving skills, to integrate and apply a wide-range of
knowledge, to collaborate, and to be self-motivated
- Discuss or explain the applications of what the students are studying so that the
students to understand the knowledge in the real-world context and realize the impact
of the knowledge on the society
- Discuss or explain recent advancement and problems in the subject to encourage the
students to be continuously seeking new knowledge and be inquisitive
- Include interesting case studies
- Continuously adapt the pace and the strategy of teaching by regularly evaluating the
students and/or obtaining feedback from the students
- Assign the students to write essays on the applications of what they are studying in
the course
##### 4.2.2.3 Assessment strategies

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
Evaluate the students’ knowledge and skills from
- Mid-term examination
- Final examination
- Regular quizzes
- Assignments (homework, term projects, etc.)
- Oral examinations

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 4.2.3 Cognitive skills

##### 4.2.3.1 Learning outcomes

1. Have learned to think analytically and systematically
2. Be able to search, interpret, and evaluate information for use in solving problems
creatively
3. Be able to gather, study, analyze, and summarize issues and requirements
4. Be able to apply knowledge and skills in solving computing related problems properly


##### 4.2.3.2 Teaching strategies

- Ask the students to write a summary or a critical analysis on some topic related to
what they are studying
- Adopt a problem-based learning strategy
- Suggest additional reading materials
- Assign the students to study the literature on some topics related to what they are
studying
- Assign homework or projects that encourage creativity, e.g., the students may be
asked to suggest their own ideas to solve a problem or to think of topics of their
projects by themselves
- Give an assignment that involves the analysis of existing systems, problems, or
requirements, and the summary of the findings
- Give an assignment that involves some real-world problem
- Reward the students for their creativity
- Give an assignment that trains the students’ research skills
- Organize a seminar and encourage the student an opportunity to raise questions and
comments and debate
##### 4.2.3.3 Assessment strategies

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
Evaluate through assignments and examinations which are designed to test the students the
following
- the ability to solve unfamiliar problems by applying what they have learned
- the ability to think outside the box and find a creative solution
- the ability to identify incorrect reasoning
- the ability to summarize the knowledge
- the ability to evaluate existing techniques, ideas, or systems, point out their pros and
cons, and offer an improvement
- the ability to come up with a creative solution or an invention
- the ability to research on advanced topics in the subject

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 4.2.4 Interpersonal skills and responsibility

##### 4.2.4.1 Learning outcomes

1. Be able to communicate in English with various groups of people effectively
2. Be able to assist and support in resolving problematic situations both in the role of a
leader and in the role of a team member
3. Be able to utilize the knowledge in the field of study to lead the society in appropriate
topics
4. Be responsible for own actions and for the team
5. Be able to suggest solutions to situations both as an individual and as part of a group,
and exhibit own point of view appropriately as an individual and also for the group
6. Be responsible for continuous development of their own knowledge and the
knowledge of their profession


##### 4.2.4.2 Teaching strategies

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
- Assign group projects or homework
- Encourage discussions in English
- Ask the student to give a presentation to the class
- Organize a seminar and encourage the student to participate in a discussion
- Assign tasks which involve gathering information from other people
- Assign homework or projects that involves important issues in the society and how to
apply the knowledge and skills that the student has learned to solve such issues
- Train communication skills
- Evaluate group assignments by group instead of individually
- Look at case studies of actual problems in the real world and ask the student to do
research and offer solutions to such problems
- Suggest additional reading materials
- Organize a visit to a company or organization to learn about its operation,
management, and its role and responsibilities within the society
- Teach the student the role and responsibility of their future profession to the society
- Encourage the student to participate in activities that involve interactions with people
from outside the class or the university
- Give a group assignment and ask each student in a group to take turn to be the leader
of the group
##### 4.2.4.3 Assessment strategies

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
- Evaluate the student’s communication skills from their presentations, oral
examination, and seminar
- Evaluate the student’s ability to gather information from other people
- Evaluate the student’s responsibility on the assigned tasks
- Evaluate the student’s ability to work as a team to accomplish the assigned tasks
under the given time and resource constraints

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 4.2.5 Analytical and communication skills

##### 4.2.5.1 Learning outcomes

1. Have acquired skills in utilizing the existing tools that are essential in computer-
related work
2. Be able to suggest solutions to problems using mathematical information or suggest
the use of applied statistics on related problems creatively
3. Be able to communicate effectively both in oral form and in written form, as well as
be able to choose appropriate forms of presentation media
4. Be able to utilize information and communications technology appropriately.
##### 4.2.5.2 Teaching strategies

- Train the quantitative reasoning and numerical skills useful for solving problem
- Assign homework or projects which requires numerical skills
- Train the skills to effectively find information using computers and the Internet


- Train the skills in preparing and delivering effective presentations on computers
- Assign the student to acquire information from various sources in an effective manner
by utilizing information technology
- Teach the students to use statistical data or other numerical techniques to support their
arguments
##### 4.2.5.3 Assessment strategies

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
- Design the exam questions and assignments to test students the following:
  - The ability to make an effective use of numerical and statistical techniques
  - The ability to make an effective use of information technology in the given tasks
  - The ability to use statistical data or other numerical techniques to support their arguments
- Evaluate the student’s ability prepare and deliver an effective presentation using
information technology

chunks.content:

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 5.1 Regulations and criteria for grading

The regulations and criteria for grading can be found in the Regulation of King Mongkut’s
Institute of Technology Ladkrabang on Undergraduate Study B.E. 2564.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 5.2.1 Verification of the standards of student achievements during study
chunks.content:
#### 5.2.1 Verification of the standards of student achievements during study

- The program management committee (or a nominated committee) checks the score
and grade report of each course before submission. If any abnormalities are spotted,
the committee notifies the course instructor to recheck and/or provide explanations,
possibly including showing of marked exam papers or homework.
- In each year, the program management committee (or a nominated committee)
conducts a verification of the standards of student achievements on selected courses
using one or more of the following methods:
- Studying the course plan and course report
- Studying marked homework, exam papers, and/or student projects
- Interviewing some students in the class
- Interviewing the course instructor

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 5.2.2 Verification of the standards of student achievements after graduation
chunks.content:
#### 5.2.2 Verification of the standards of student achievements after graduation
- Request for feedbacks from the employers of the recent graduates
- Interview the recent graduates and the graduates who have been working for 2 years
or more

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 5.3 Graduation requirements

To be awarded a degree from KMITL, the student must satisfy all the graduation
requirements specified in the Regulation of King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology
Ladkrabang on Undergraduate Study B.E. 2564

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 6.1 Preparatory activities for new academic staff members
chunks.content:
### 6.1 Preparatory activities for new academic staff members

- Issuing (or assisting in issuing) all necessary documents for new academic staff,
including a staff identification card, and the computer accounts
- Requiring a new academic staff member to attend the orientation session organized by
the university
- Informing the new academic staff member their responsibilities and details on the
performance evaluation
- Introducing the new academic staff member to all existing members of the
management staff, academic staff and supporting staff
- Assigning a current staff member to act as a mentor for a new academic staff member
- Providing a computer and teach the new academic staff member on using the IT
services within the university

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 6.2.1 Development of skills in teaching, assessment, and evaluation
chunks.content:
#### 6.2.1 Development of skills in teaching, assessment, and evaluation

- Recommend the lecturers a website and other resources which provides useful
information on the development of skills in teaching, assessment, and evaluation
- Encourage and support all academic staff members to attend workshops, seminars, or
courses related to the development of skills in teaching, assessment, and evaluation
held within the university or outside
- Organize a lecturer meeting every semester and invite all lecturers to join and share
any problem, comment, or good practice

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 6.2.2 Academic and professional development

- Encourage and support academic staff members to:
  - Work with the industry
  - Collaborate with academic staff in a partner university in Thailand and abroad
  - Attend international conferences in their respective fields of expertise at least once a year
  - Apply for research grants
  - Publish their research work in international journals
  - Patent their research works
- Provide the staff with news and information that are useful to their academic and
professional development, including workshops, seminar or other events,
scholarships, etc.
- Partner with other universities to enable opportunities for research collaborations and
staff exchange

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
The program adopts the CUPT Quality Assurance (CUPT QA) system initiated by the
Council of the University President of Thailand. CUPT QA consists of two components, each
specifying a set of indicators that the program must satisfy. The first component consists of
the indicators which ensure that the program is operated in accordance with the standards set
out by the Thai Qualifications Framework for Higher Education (TQF). The second
component consists of several indicators adopted from the AUN Quality Assurance (AUN
QA) system, initiated by ASEAN University Network. The program is subject to a regular
quality assurance evaluation. The program is evaluated against the first component of CUPT
QA every year and against the second component at least once every 5 years. The following groups of individuals are involved in the quality assurance process:
1. Curriculum revision committee is responsible for revising the curriculum of the
program in accordance with the requirements of TQF and CUPT QA. The committee
must include at least 3 members who are experts in computer engineering or a related
field, one of which should come from the industry. 2. Program management committee consists of the five program faculty members. The
committee is responsible for managing the program and preparing the Self-
Assessment Report (SAR) every year for quality assurance evaluation. 3. Program stakeholders are the groups of individuals with potential interest in the
program.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
The following groups of individuals have been identified as the stakeholders
of the program:
a. Students: the students who were studying in the program
b. Lecturers: the academic staff members who delivered or assisted in delivering
courses in the program
c. Alumni: the students who graduated from the program
d. Employers: the current or prospective employers of the graduates of the
program. 4. Quality assurance evaluators are responsible for evaluating the program in the
(annual) quality assurance evaluation. The evaluators must be qualified to evaluate the
program according to CUPT QA.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
The goal of the program is to produce capable computer engineers who could work in the
industry at the international level and be able to pursue a postgraduate study on advanced
topics in a computing-related field. As specified in the Qualification Standards for
Undergraduate Programs in Computing (TQF 1 - Computing) announced by the Ministry of
Education of Thailand, the graduates are expected to have the following characteristics:
1. Having morals and ethics; being humble; being a good citizen; being responsible for
oneself, the profession, and the society
2. Having basic knowledge in software engineering, both theoretically and practically at
a sufficiently good level; Being able to properly apply the knowledge in the
profession and for further study
3. Having up-to-date knowledge; Being inquisitive; Being able to further own
knowledge continually to improve oneself, the jobs, and the society
4. Can think and can do; Being able to choose a solution systematically and
appropriately
5. Having teamworking skills and management skills
6. Being able acquire new knowledge; Being able to communicate with others
effectively
7. Being proficient in the use of English language for communication; Being able to
utilize information technology effectively
8. Being able to analyze the user’s requirements and able to design, develop, deploy, and
improve computer software to meet the user’s requirements and so that it works
effectively within its working environment
9.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
Being able to analyze the impact of an application of computers on people,
organizations, and society, including the impact in the legal and ethical aspects
10. Being able to act as consultants on the use computer systems in organizations
11. Being able to manage information systems within organizations
12. Being able to develop small-scale computer programs of real use
Additionally, the graduates are expected to have the special characteristics stated in Section
4.1. These expected characteristics have been translated in the learning outcomes in the five
domains of learning as listed in Section 4.2. These learning outcomes act as a basis in the
design of the curriculum. Being one of the stakeholders, the graduates act as an integral component of CUPT QA. The
graduates are involved the quality assurance process in the following ways:
- Feedbacks from the graduates are used in the revision of the curriculum and are
reported as part of the annual program management report. - The capabilities of the graduates affect their performance in their jobs, which is
reported by their employers in the (annual) employer survey. - In the long run, the graduate capability affects the reputation of the program which, in
turn, affects the employability of the graduates, one of the indicators in CUPT QA.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 7.3 Students

Students are a major element in CUPT quality assurance process. The program is required to
have a clear and effective student intake policy and a system for monitoring students’
academic performance. The student graduation and failure rates and the student’s satisfaction
level are constantly monitored and considered when reviewing the program.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 7.3.1 Student intakes

The methods and criteria for admitting students have been chosen with the following goals:
- The admitted students have sufficient knowledge and skills to succeed in the program.
- The admitted students show interest in software engineering, or computing and
technology in general, are motivated to succeed, and understand the possible career
paths once they graduate. Experiences in computer programming are beneficial, but
not required.
- Students from various educational background (e.g., Thai schools, International
Schools, homeschool/non-formal education, etc.) both within Thailand and abroad can
apply and be considered.
- The application and selection of students should not be unnecessarily complicated and
costly, while still being effective.
To open the admission to students with different backgrounds, students are admitted through
at least two different channels, as summarized in the table below.

| Channel | Target Students | Qualifications to Consider |
|---|---|---|
| Quota Admission | Students with excellent records and/or special abilities from international schools, English programs, Thai schools, and abroad | 1. English language test score; 2. Test score or academic records on mathematical skills |
| National Central Admission | Thai high school graduates | Scores from national qualification tests |

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 7.3.2 Student monitoring

From entering the university, each student is assigned an academic advisor who monitors the
student’s academic performance and provides advice on academic matters as well as any
matter that can potentially affects their study. Each academic advisor sets aside office hours
to offer consultations.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 7.3.3 Graduation and failure rates

As part of the quality assurance evaluation, the graduation and failure rates are constantly
monitored and reported every year. All records of students’ termination of study (either
withdrawal or failure) are kept and studied by the program management committee.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 7.3.4 Student satisfaction

Student satisfaction is feedbacked to the program management committee through the
course/lecturer satisfaction survey held at the end of every semester and the program
satisfaction.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 7.4 Academic staff

Quality of academic staff is a crucial factor for the program to succeed. The Ministry of
Education of Thailand has enacted criteria on the qualifications of academic staff members in
universities and the requirements on the academic staff involved in a program. CUPT QA
stipulates further quality indicators regarding the academic staff in a program.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 7.4.1 Qualifications and quantity of academic staff
chunks.content:
#### 7.4.1 Qualifications and quantity of academic staff

- There are at least 5 program faculty members in the program. Each program faculty
member must have an academic background related to the program, have a master’s
degree or higher or be in the position of Assistant Professor or higher, and have at
least one acceptable academic publication in the past 5 years.
- A lecturer for a course in the program can either be a permanent academic staff
member in the university or be a visiting lecturer from outside the university. Every
lecturer must have a master’s degree or higher or be in the position of Assistant
Professor or higher in the field that related to the course. A visiting lecturer with only
a bachelor’s degree may be allowed to teach in the program only if he/she has at least
6 years of work or teaching experience in the relevant topics.
- All lecturers are required to have proficient English language skills.
- The ratio of the number of students per one (permanent) academic staff member is
expected to be at 10 or lower.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 7.4.2 Expected workload and responsibilities of academic staff
chunks.content:
#### 7.4.2 Expected workload and responsibilities of academic staff

- As a guideline, each academic staff member in the program is expected to teach 3 –
12 hours per week. In addition, each academic staff member is expected to be
involved in advising students projects, typically 1 – 5 project groups in a semester.
- All academic staff members are expected to be active in research and/or academic
services to external organizations in the area that are related to their fields of expertise
or the courses that they teach. This is to ensure that they have up-to-date knowledge in
the relevant fields.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 7.5 Program, teaching and learning, and student evaluation
chunks.content:
### 7.5 Program, teaching and learning, and student evaluation

- The program management committee whose members are the program faculty
members are responsible for managing the program. One member of the committee
takes the position of the program director, another as the deputy director. The
management committee is supported by an administrative staff member, who acts as
the secretary to the committee.
- The program takes into accounts the feedbacks from 4 groups of stakeholders, namely
employers, alumni, academic staff, and students, to define appropriate program
learning outcomes (PLO)s. The definition of course learning outcomes (CLOs) will be
based on the PLO breakdown. The appropriate lecturer for each course will be then
chosen from its CLOs.
- Before each semester begins, each lecturer is required to revise and submit the course
specification (in TQF 3 format), containing the course plan and assessment plan for
that semester. The program management committee will check each submitted course
specification and contact the lecturer if the committee notice problems or have
suggestions for improvements.
- After the end of the semester, each lecturer is required to prepare and submit a course
report (in TQF 5 format), which summarizes the delivery of the course in that
semester.
- This includes a summary of the grade report, a summary of the student evaluation of
the course and the lecturer, and a summary of the problems occurred and a plan for
improvements

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 7.6 Teaching and learning resources

The government annual expenditure and program income are used to support education
management and quality improvement, such as to acquire teaching resources, audio visual
equipment, as well as other necessities. Parts of the budget will be allocated to academic staff
development.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 7.6.1 Existing teaching and learning resources.

List of existing teaching and learning resources can be found in the original PDF (page 242).

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 7.6.2 Acquisition of additional resources

The program management committee are responsible for the planning, acquisition, and
maintenance of the teaching and learning resources to ensure that they are sufficient and are
in good working order.
