chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
Evaluation Criteria
To conform to the standards of the Thai Qualification Framework for Higher Education, the
program must satisfy the criteria of all the mandatory indicators (Indicator No. 1 - 5) and at
least 80% of the total number of indicators specified in each academic year.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
#### 8.1.1 Evaluation of teaching strategies

- The lecturers and the program management committee study the student evaluation of
courses and lecturers to obtain feedbacks on the effectiveness of the teaching
strategies.
- The lecturers and the program management committee study the students’ scores to
determine whether the teaching strategies used were effective or not.
- The program management committee regularly organizes a knowledge-sharing
session for the lecturers to meet and share the experiences and opinions on different
teaching strategies used.
- The program management committee interviews the lecturer of a course which has
some other course as a pre-requisite to ask whether the students have sufficient
background knowledge and skills from the pre-requisite courses. If not, there could be
problems with the teaching strategies used in the pre-requisite courses.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 8.1.2 Evaluation of the lecturers’ skills in executing teaching strategies
chunks.content:
#### 8.1.2 Evaluation of the lecturers’ skills in executing teaching strategies

- The lecturers and the program management committee study the student evaluation of
courses and lecturers to obtain feedbacks on the lecturers’ skills in executing teaching
strategies.
- The lecturers and the program management committee study the students’ scores to
determine whether the lecturers have sufficient skills in executing teaching strategies
- The program management committee interviews the lecturer of a course which has
some other course as a pre-requisite to ask whether the students have sufficient
background knowledge and skills from the pre-requisite courses. If not, there could be
problems with the teaching skills of the lecturers of the pre-requisite courses.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.content:
### 8.2 Overall evaluation of the program

The program is subject to the CUPT quality assurance process, which include the criteria in
all aspects of the program execution. The program is evaluated against the first part of CUPT
QA (containing the criteria required by the Office of the Higher Education Commission)
every year, and against all criteria in both parts of CUPT QA at least once every 5 years. The
evaluation committee includes qualified evaluators who are experts in computer engineering
or related fields.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 8.3 Evaluation of program execution with respect to the program specification
chunks.content:
### 8.3 Evaluation of program execution with respect to the program specification
The program management committee and the quality assurance evaluators evaluate the
program execution in each academic year with respect to the KPIs specified in Section
7.7 of this document.

chunks.title: Cei Curriculum 20220222
chunks.section: 8.4 Review of evaluation results and planning for improvements
chunks.content:
### 8.4 Review of evaluation results and planning for improvements

The program management committee summarizes the operation of the program in each
academic year in the annual program report and the QA self-assessment report. These reports
contain a summary of the problems occurred, feedbacks from various groups of stakeholders,
as well as statistics on the students and the graduates. The committee uses this information,
together with comment and suggestions from the quality assurance evaluators, to revise the
program and/or plan for improvements.

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.section: CEI Curriculum Amendment — GenEd Faculty Requirement Cancelled (2023)
chunks.content:
# CEI Curriculum Amendment — GenEd Faculty Requirement Cancelled (2023)

> 📄 [View original PDF](documents/cei-gened-amendment-20230615.pdf) — source of truth  
> 📅 Dated: 21 June 2023 (B.E. 2566) — Faculty Academic Committee Resolution No. 4/2566  
> ⚠️ This is an unofficial English translation. The original document is in Thai. OCR text is included below for reference.

---

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
## Summary

The Faculty of Engineering Academic Committee resolved that **CEI students may now enroll in ANY course from the General Education (GenEd) category** to satisfy the faculty-mandated course requirement, effective for the **2022 curriculum (B.E. 2565)** version and onwards.

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
### What Changed

| Before | After |
|--------|-------|
| Students had to take specific GenEd courses from a faculty-mandated list | Students can pick **any** GenEd course from the updated 2025 catalog |

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
### Who This Affects

Only the **Computer Engineering International Program (CEI)** — B.Eng. in Computer Engineering (International Program), curriculum version B.E. 2565 (2022).

Other programs in the same resolution (Robotics & AI, Electrical/Communications, Mechanical, Software Engineering) had separate unrelated amendments (prerequisite fixes, elective additions).

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
### Key Detail

Section 3.1.2 of the curriculum structure, General Education category, is amended with the following footnote:

> "Students may enroll in any course from the General Education category (revised B.E. 2565) in place of the Faculty Course Requirement courses."

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
### Effective Date

Approved by the Faculty Academic Committee on **21 June 2023** (meeting 4/2566) and forwarded to the Institute Academic Council for final approval.

---

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
## Raw OCR Text (Thai — for reference)

The original PDF contained only scanned Thai text. Below is the machine-extracted OCR output. Expect errors in spacing and character recognition.

chunks.title: Cei Gened Amendment 20230615
chunks.content:
> มติคณะกรรมการสภาวิชาการ ครั้งที่ 4/2566  
> เรื่อง ขอความเห็นชอบการปรับปรุงแก้ไขหลักสูตร (กรณีไม่กระทบกระเทือนโครงสร้าง) คณะวิศวกรรมศาสตร์  
>  
> คณะวิศวกรรมศาสตร์มีความประสงค์เสนอขอความเห็นชอบการปรับปรุงแก้ไขหลักสูตร (กรณีไม่กระทบกระเทือนโครงสร้าง) ทั้งนี้สำนักงานบริหารวิชาการและคุณภาพการศึกษาได้ตรวจสอบหลักสูตรแล้วเป็นไปตามเกณฑ์มาตรฐานหลักสูตรระดับปริญญาตรี พ.ศ. 2555 / พ.ศ. 2548 ซึ่งสาระของการขอปรับปรุงเป็นการปรับปรุงแบบไม่กระทบกระเทือนโครงสร้าง โดยจำนวนหน่วยกิตรวมไม่เปลี่ยนแปลง  
>  
> จึงเสนอขอความเห็นชอบการปรับปรุงแก้ไขหลักสูตร (กรณีไม่กระทบกระเทือนโครงสร้าง) จำนวน 4 หลักสูตร ดังนี้  
>  
> **ระดับปริญญาตรี**  
> 1. หลักสูตรวิศวกรรมศาสตรบัณฑิต สาขาวิชาวิศวกรรมหุ่นยนต์และปัญญาประดิษฐ์ (หลักสูตรนานาชาติ) (ฉบับปี พ.ศ. 2561)  
> 2. หลักสูตรวิศวกรรมศาสตรบัณฑิต สาขาวิชาวิศวกรรมไฟฟ้าสื่อสารและเครือข่าย (ฉบับปี พ.ศ. 2562)  
> 3. หลักสูตรวิศวกรรมศาสตรบัณฑิต สาขาวิชาวิศวกรรมเครื่องกล (หลักสูตรนานาชาติ) (ฉบับปี พ.ศ. 2565)  
> 4. **หลักสูตรวิศวกรรมศาสตรบัณฑิต สาขาวิชาวิศวกรรมคอมพิวเตอร์ (หลักสูตรนานาชาติ) (ฉบับปี พ.ศ. 2565)**  
>  
> **สำหรับ CEI (ลำดับที่ 4):**  
> เหตุผลการปรับปรุง: แก้ไขข้อกำหนดของรายวิชากลุ่มวิชาตามเกณฑ์ของคณะ (Faculty Course Requirement) เพื่อประโยชน์ในการจบการศึกษาของนักศึกษา  
>  
> สาระในการปรับปรุงแก้ไข: ข้อ 3.1.2 โครงสร้างหลักสูตร ก. หมวดศึกษาทั่วไป ให้เพิ่มข้อความแนบท้าย:  
> **"หมายเหตุ: ให้นักศึกษาสามารถลงทะเบียนรายวิชาในหมวดวิชาศึกษาทั่วไป (ฉบับปรับปรุง พ.ศ.

chunks.title: Investment Course Policies 20260630
chunks.content:
## Course Description

Study time value of money, investment planning principles, types, return and risk of each asset class, finance theories related to investment, risk management, financial instrument analysis, fundamental of valuation, and portfolio management.

---

chunks.title: Investment Course Policies 20260630
chunks.content:
## Course Learning Outcomes (CLO)

1. Students understand and become familiar with various asset classes including stocks, bonds, gold, ETFs, and global equity mutual funds, and understand the risks associated with each asset class.
2. Students can apply investment planning for themselves in real life.
3. Students can search for information on economics, finance, investing in stocks, mutual funds, and alternative assets.
4. Students can choose to invest in stocks or mutual funds around the world to suit themselves to get a return worth the risk.
5. Students have a good mindset for investing — don't stress about short-term fluctuations and can correct errors.

---

chunks.title: Investment Course Policies 20260630
chunks.content:

| GE-LO | Description | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| GE-LO 1 | Analytical and Critical Thinking | 2 |
| GE-LO 2 | Complex Problem Solving | 2 |
| GE-LO 5 | Integrity and Perseverance | 4 |
| GE-LO 6 | Active Learning and Learning Strategies | 4 |
| GE-LO 10 | Entrepreneurship and Startup | 3 |
| **Total** | | **15** |

**Career Readiness:** ☒ Investor

---

chunks.content:

| In-Class Presentation No.1 (Group) | Throughout | 13% | 8 |
| Class Worksheets (Individual/Group) | Throughout | 13% | 8 |
| Assignment No.1 — Fixed Income | Before Midterm (dist. 23 Jul) | 10% | 6 |
| Assignment No.2 — Common Stocks | After Midterm (dist. 27 Aug) | 10% | 6 |
| In-Class Presentation No.2 (Group) | Week 12–14 | 20% | 12 |
| Final Exam (Must Attend) | Week 15 | 20% | 12 |
| **Total** | | **100%** | **60** |

> Attendance less than 80% (miss 3+ classes) → forfeits right to take the exam. No final exam → grade F.

---

chunks.content:

| 2 | 9 Jul | Investment risk analysis — external/internal factors, suitability test |

| 5 | 30 Jul | Alternative investments (real estate, gold, infrastructure, crypto, forex) |

| — | 17–23 Aug | **Midterm Exam Week** (no midterm for this subject) |
| 8 | 27 Aug | Stock valuation: P/E, P/BV, P/S, EV/EBITDA, DCF, DDM |
| 9 | 3 Sep | Technical analysis — concepts, support/resistance, TradingView |
| 10 | 10 Sep | Portfolio management — diversification, return/risk, rebalancing, core-satellite |
| 11 | 17 Sep | Investment channels — mutual funds/ETFs, tax planning |
| 12 | 24 Sep | Implement 6 investment planning steps, portfolio design workshop |
| 13 | 1 Oct | Workshop: stock selection, mock portfolio report |
| 14 | 8 Oct | Presentation 1 |
| — | 12–16 Oct | Bangkok Special Holidays |
| 15 | 22 Oct | Final Exam |

| 6 | 27 Aug | Derivatives |

| 8 | 17 Sep | Alternative Investments 2 — REIT/Digital & Cryptocurrency |
| 9 | 17 Sep | Portfolio Management |

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
# Investment Lec01 — Introduction to Investing (2026)

> 📄 [View original PDF](documents/investment-lec01-intro-investing-20260702.pdf) — source of truth  
> ⚠️ The original lecture slides are mostly image-based — many diagrams, charts, and illustrations were not extractable as text. The content below has been supplemented with context where needed to make this summary self-contained.

Investment Planning (96643026)
2 July 2026

**Instructor:** Ajarn Dr. Sakdipat Kritsanachinda — [sakdipat.kr@kmitl.ac.th](mailto:sakdipat.kr@kmitl.ac.th)

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
## Communication & Class Rules

- We will use **MS Teams** and/or **Email** to communicate. No Line group.
- **Attendance** checked weekly. 15 mins late allowed; after 15 mins = late.
- Absence → fill out leave form and submit to instructor's email.
- Raise your hand to speak. Turn on cameras during online classes.
- Be interactive and respect others.

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
## About This Module

- Focus on understanding the **rationale behind investment**.
- Understanding **terminology** and **calculations** is important.
- Regularly follow the news; listening to and reading analysis helps make better decisions.
- Don't believe everything you read — listen to opinions carefully, be comprehensive, make your own decisions.
- **The money is yours.** The decision to buy/sell is your own. Take responsibility.

> ⚠️ Investment is risky. Investors should understand the characteristics of assets and study information before deciding to invest.

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
## Course Outline

| Week | Date | Topic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 Jul | Course Syllabus and What is Investment? |
| 2 | 9 Jul | Risks and Rewards of Personal Budgeting |

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.section: 1. What is an Investment? Why Invest?
chunks.content:
## 1. What is an Investment? Why Invest?

**Investment** is the use of accumulated money to generate higher returns than savings — investing in assets or instruments such as stocks, mutual funds, real estate, cryptocurrency, gold, collectibles, NFTs, etc.

> **Investing is riskier than saving.**

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
### How to Reduce Investment Risk

1. Possess investment knowledge
2. Understand yourself
3. Discipline in investment (cutting losses)
4. Have a good investment plan

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
## Source of Income

> 📄 See [PDF page 29](documents/investment-lec01-intro-investing-20260702.pdf#page=29) — income sources diagram.

There are broadly two types of income:

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Income** | You trade time for money — stop working, income stops | Salary, freelance, business profits you personally run |
| **Passive Income** | Money works for you — income flows without active daily effort | Dividends, rental income, interest, royalties, capital gains |

The goal of investing is to build **passive income streams** so you're not dependent on trading your time for money forever.

> 💡 **Getting rich before old age, or get old before you get rich?** — The earlier you start investing, the more time compound interest has to work in your favour.

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
### The Financial Pyramid / Triangle A common framework for prioritising your money: ```mermaid graph BT SAFETY["SAFETY
Cash reserves, bank deposits,
money market"] PROTECTION["PROTECTION
Insurance (health, life)
+ emergency fund (6–12 months)"] GROWTH["GROWTH
Invest for wealth
(stocks, mutual funds, real estate)"] SAFETY --> PROTECTION PROTECTION --> GROWTH ``` 1. **Safety layer:** Build cash reserves first (3–6 months of expenses in liquid savings) 2. **Protection layer:** Get adequate insurance coverage + 6–12 months emergency fund 3. **Growth layer:** Invest remaining funds for long-term wealth creation > 📄 See the PDF for the full Financial Triangle diagram. ---

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
## Summary

Investment is to **delay spending today to buy assets**. It aims to receive **cash flow** and **value increase** — to be worth the opportunity cost, compensate for inflation, and compensate for risk.

chunks.title: Investment Lec01 Intro Investing 20260702
chunks.content:
### Lesson 1 Recommended Areas:

1. What is Investing? Why Invest?
2. Time value of money, investment options
3. Know Equities – Debt Instruments
4. Check your status before you start investing — Financial Triangle — aim to generate passive income to create wealth

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
## 1. Status Check Before Investing

> 💬 **Warren Buffett:** "Don't invest in something you don't understand."
>
> 📄 See [PDF page 3](documents/investment-lec02-personal-finance-risk-20260709.pdf#page=3) — Warren Buffett quote.

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.section: Check Your Financial Status Before You Start Investing
chunks.content:
### Check Your Financial Status Before You Start Investing

> 📄 See [PDF page 7](documents/investment-lec02-personal-finance-risk-20260709.pdf#page=7) — financial status checklist diagram.

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Money Management Guidelines ```mermaid graph BT EMERGENCY["6–12 months savings
+ health/life insurance
(emergency fund)"] RETIRE["Minimum savings:
retirement money"] LT["Long-term investment
for quality of life
(can be more risky, bonus)"] EMERGENCY --> RETIRE RETIRE --> LT ``` **Income > Expenses** → Emergency Reserves → Difference (invest) **Income − Savings = Expenses** — Set a goal for retirement.

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Key Principles

- **Invest ≠ Speculate**
- Risk Management
- Diversification
- Financial discipline
- Pay attention and follow relevant news

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Sources of News & Information

To stay informed as an investor, regularly follow financial news from reliable sources:

| Source | Type |
|---|---|
| Bloomberg, CNBC, Reuters | International financial news |
| Stock Exchange of Thailand (SET) | Thai market data, listed company info |
| SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) | Regulatory filings, fund info |
| Bank of Thailand | Monetary policy, interest rates, economic data |
| Investing.com, TradingView | Market data, charts, technical analysis |
| Yahoo Finance, Finnomena, Jitta | Portfolio tracking, stock analysis (Thai market) |
| Dime, Settrade | Trading platforms popular in Thailand |

> 📄 See [PDF pages 4–6](documents/investment-lec02-personal-finance-risk-20260709.pdf#page=4) — sources of news slides.

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Starting Out

**Debt Management:**
- School loans, automobiles, house

**Cash Reserves:**
- 3–6 months of net income in cash, money market, or short-term bond fund

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Debt Benchmarks

- Monthly housing costs (principal, interest, taxes, insurance) should be **≤ 28% of gross income**.

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Debt Pitfalls Early in Career

- Too much house → you may have to move
- Too much car → you don't deserve it
- Too much credit card debt → restaurants, vacations, furniture

> Mortgage debt's interest is tax deductible and hopefully your home will appreciate.

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Investment Assets Overview

| Asset Class | Key Characteristics |
|---|---|
| **Bank Deposits** | Return ~1%, negligible risk, liquid as cash, cannot beat inflation |
| **Fixed Income / Bonds** | Historically ~5–6% return. Short-term (≤1yr), Intermediate (2–10yr), Long-term (>10yr). Affected by interest rate, reinvestment, default, and inflation risks. |
| **Equities / Common Shares** | Most volatile — greatest risk, greatest rewards. S&P 500 ~12.02% since 1980. Thai SET50 ~3.44% since 1995. |
| **Alternative Assets** | Real estate, REITs, precious metals, digital currencies, commodities |

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Bank Deposits / Money Markets

- Return is low (~1%) but risk is negligible
- Most funds consist of Thai Govt. Bonds and U.S. Treasury issues / AAA corporate securities
- Maturity < 270 days
- Considered as liquid as cash
- **Cannot beat inflation**

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Bonds

- Historically returned ~5–6%
- Categories: Short term (≤1 yr), Intermediate (2–10 yr), Long term (>10 yr)
- Credit grade affects price and return (Treasury = highest grade, lowest return; junk bonds = lowest grade, highest return/risk)
- Affected by: interest rate risk, reinvestment rate risk, default risk, purchasing power/inflation risk
- If selected carefully, **can beat inflation**

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Equities

- Primarily common stock
- Best performing asset class over the past 20 years (especially in low inflation)
- Dividends have become less important
- Most volatile (greatest risk, greatest rewards)
- S&P 500: ~12.02% annualized since 1980 (with several crashes)
- Thai SET50: ~3.44% annualized since 1995; lost ~0.5% per year in past 5 years

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
#### Types of Stocks

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Growth** | Fast-growing companies, limited equity, rapidly increasing cash | Amazon, Google, THG, CPF |
| **Value** | Older companies, may have dividends, not currently in favor | General Electric, PTT, SCC |
| **Equity Income** | Good dividends, stable companies | BATS, PTTEP |

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
#### Hedge Funds

- Use exotic instruments, tend to short markets and use leverage
- Did well during bubble bursting but can be "squeezed"
- High net worth individuals only — managers take first 20% of profit

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Alternative Investments — Real Estate

- Your home (usually a first investment)
- Rental property (headache unless professionally managed)
- **REITs** (Real Estate Investment Trusts): equity or mortgage based, must pay out 90% of annual income, can diversify portfolio

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Digital Currencies & Precious Metals

- Gold was significant in international currency stability pre-1974 (Bretton Woods)
- Free global markets have replaced the gold standard
- Platinum and silver are commodities rather than investment vehicles
- ⚠️ A lot of risks involved — be careful!

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Definitions

- **Risk:** The uncertainty that may occur in the future — possibility of mistakes, damages, or adverse events.
- **Investment Risk:** The uncertainty or possibility of not receiving the expected return.

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Five Categories of Risk

| Risk Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Market Risk** | Changes in overall market conditions affecting all asset prices (e.g., sluggish domestic economy) |
| **Business Risk** | Nature of the business — industry segment factors or company's internal operations affecting profitability |
| **Liquidity Risk** | Inability to sell assets immediately when cash is needed, or having to lose money to convert to cash |
| **Interest Rate Risk** | Fluctuations in market interest rates affecting asset prices and desired return levels |
| **Inflation Risk** | Purchasing power erosion — returns less than inflation reduce real value of money |

> **"High Risk, High (Expected) Return"** — High risk means possibility of greater profit, but also chance of large losses.

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Risk from an Economic Perspective

Risk = Volatility, measured by **Standard Deviation (S.D.)**

- High S.D. = Very risky
- Low S.D. = Less risk

> 📄 See [PDF page 32](documents/investment-lec02-personal-finance-risk-20260709.pdf#page=32) — Global Risk Rankings diagram.

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Components of Return

| Component | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Cash Flow** | Interest, Dividends, Rent |
| **Price Differentials** | Capital Gain / Loss |

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
#### (1) Realized Return — HPR (Holding Period Return)

Returns received over the investment period, regardless of holding period:

```
HPR = (End-of-Period Value − Initial Investment + Dividends) / Initial Investment
```

**Example:** Buy 1,000 ABC shares at 60 THB (60,000 THB). Receive 2 THB/share dividend annually for 5 years (2,000 × 5 = 10,000 THB). Sell all at 65 THB/share (65,000 THB).

```
HPR = (65,000 − 60,000 + 10,000) / 60,000 = 25%
```

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### External Factors (we have no control)

**PESTEL Analysis:** Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental

Includes: economic conditions, social trends, political stability, technology changes, extreme events (COVID-19, inflation, war)

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
### Internal Factors

Business performance (profit growth/loss vs. analyst), business expansion, business outlook, SWOT, Five Forces, executive quality

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
## 6. Self-Assessment: Know Your Risk Tolerance

Before investing, you should understand your own risk profile. A **suitability test** (risk tolerance questionnaire) helps determine:

- Your **investment horizon** — how long can you leave money invested?
- Your **risk capacity** — how much loss can you financially absorb?
- Your **risk tolerance** — how comfortable are you emotionally with volatility?
- Your **investment objective** — growth, income, or capital preservation?

Based on results, investors are typically classified as:

| Profile | Approach | Suitable Assets |
|---|---|---|
| **Conservative** | Low risk, capital preservation | Bank deposits, money market, government bonds |
| **Moderate** | Balanced risk-reward | Mix of bonds and blue-chip equities, mutual funds |
| **Aggressive** | High risk, high growth potential | Growth stocks, derivatives, crypto, alternative assets |

> 📄 See the PDF for the full self-assessment form.

---

> 💬 **Albert Einstein (attributed):** "Compound interest is the 8th wonder of the world."
>
> 📄 See [PDF page 52](documents/investment-lec02-personal-finance-risk-20260709.pdf#page=52) — compound interest quote.

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
## The Magic Number: Rule of 72

> 📄 See [PDF page 57](documents/investment-lec02-personal-finance-risk-20260709.pdf#page=57) — Rule of 72 diagram.

To estimate how many years it takes to **double your money**:

```
Years to double = 72 / Annual Return Rate (%)
```

**Reverse:** To find the return needed to double in N years:

```
Required Return = 72 / N
```

---

chunks.title: Investment Lec02 Personal Finance Risk 20260709
chunks.content:
## Summary

- Investment risk = return not as expected — could be better or worse — measured by S.D.
- HPR = actual/realized return; Arithmetic & Geometric mean for average returns
- Rule of 72 for estimating doubling time
- Internal factors: business performance, expansion, SWOT, Five Forces, executives
- External factors: PESTEL — economic, social, political, technology

chunks.content:
### Normal Rice Cooker

- Spring-loaded mechanism
- Simple on/off operation
- Cooks rice only

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### Smart Rice Cooker

- Microcontroller programmed
- Multiple modes: stew, porridge, etc.
- Precise temperature and timing control

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
## Embedded Systems

- Embedded system is the equipment that has computer inside — focus on controlling, not computing.
- e.g. electric consumer products
- Ubiquitous, invisible
- Hidden (computer inside)
- Dedicated purpose

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### 1960s — Early Milestones

- **1961** — Charles Stark Draper developed an integrated circuit to reduce the size and weight of the Apollo Guidance Computer at MIT. The first computer to use ICs, it helped astronauts collect real-time flight data.
- **1965** — Autonetics (now part of Boeing) developed the D-17B, the computer used in missile guidance systems. Widely recognized as the first mass-produced embedded system.
- **1968** — The first embedded system for a vehicle was released; the Volkswagen 1600 used a microprocessor to control its electronic fuel injection system.

> 📷 DSKEY input module alongside the Apollo Guidance Computer main casing.  
> 📷 Autonetics D-17 guidance computer from a Minuteman I missile.

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### 1970s — First Microcontrollers & Microprocessors

- **1971** — Texas Instruments developed the TMS1000, the first microcontroller
  - Commercial in 1974
  - 4-bit, ROM, RAM, $2 in bulk order
- **1971** — Intel released commercially the 4004, 4-bit microprocessor used in calculators and small electronics
- **1972** — Intel released the 8080, 8-bit microprocessor
- **1978** — Intel released the 8086, 16-bit microprocessor — started the x86 series

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### 1980s–1990s — Embedded Operating Systems

- **1987** — First embedded operating system, the real-time VxWorks, released by Wind River
- **1996** — Microsoft released Windows Embedded CE
- **Late 1990s** — First embedded Linux products began to appear

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630

chunks.content:

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### Vending Machine

- One of the good examples of embedded systems
- Single microcontroller as the main component
- **Input devices:** keypad and coin counter module
- **Output devices:** LCD display and gate actuator
- **Communication:** 3G/4G

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### Segway

- Personal transporter device
- Uses gyroscopes to maintain horizontal balance
- When gyroscopes detect imbalance, motor rotates to move Segway forward proportional to the measured data — preventing the fall
- When balance is restored, motor stops rotating
- Composed of microcontroller, sensors, and actuators
- [Gyroscope operation (GIF)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Gyroscope_operation.gif)

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### Closed Loop Control Systems
- Use sensor data to achieve accurate control
- Compare the setpoint and the actual point
- e.g. heater element in the oven
- More accurate
- Need fast microcontroller

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### Open Loop Control Systems
- Don't use sensor data
- Use calibrated actuator
- e.g. motor speed control with input voltage
- Uncomplicated

---

## Microcontroller

  - **Processor core**
  - **Memory**
  - **Input/Output (Peripheral)**
- Main application is to control
- Examples: PIC, 8051, ARM7TDMI, Cortex

chunks.content:
### Development Process

- Write the source code
- Compile the source code into binary machine code

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### The Acorn Era (1980s)

- **1981** — Computers produced by Acorn Computer became popular in schools and universities in the UK
- Acorn used the 6502, an 8-bit microprocessor made by MOS Technology
- Same year, IBM produced its first PCs using a more powerful Intel 16-bit microprocessor, the 8088
- Around the 1980s, the influence of IBM PC grew, and its smaller competitors began to fade
- Despite Acorn's UK success, it did not export well

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### The Pivot to IP Licensing

Acorn designers' realization:

- They had the capability to design the microprocessor
- Their future might not lie in selling the completed computer itself
- They didn't need to manufacture silicon — sell IP (Intellectual Property) instead
- So they established **Advanced RISC Machines** (later called ARM Holdings) to accomplish this

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
### Acquisitions

- **2016** — SoftBank acquired ARM for $31 Billion USD
  - [The Verge article](http://www.theverge.com/2016/9/5/12798302/softbank-arm-acquisition-complete)
- **2020** — Nvidia proposed to buy ARM for $40 Billion
  - **Deal terminated in Feb 2022**
  - [NYTimes: Nvidia Deal to Buy Arm Is Off](https://www.nytimes.com) — *Nvidia Deal to Buy Arm From SoftBank Is Off After Setbacks*
  - [Bloomberg: Nvidia Prepares to Abandon $40 Billion Arm Bid](https://www.bloomberg.com)

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
## Development with STM32CubeMX

- Intuitive STM32 microcontroller selection
- Microcontroller graphical configuration:
  - Pinout with automatic conflict resolution
  - Clock tree with dynamic validation of configuration
  - Peripherals and middleware functional modes and initialization with dynamic validation of parameter constraints
  - Power sequence with estimate of consumption results
- C code project generation covering STM32 microcontroller initialization compliant with IAR, Keil, and GCC compilers
- Available as a standalone software running on Windows, Linux, and macOS

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
## Toolchains

- Cross platform toolchain
- Editor / Compiler / Linker / Debugger

- [Keil MDK](http://www.keil.com/)
- [STM32CubeIDE](https://www.st.com/en/development-tools/stm32cubeide.html)

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec01 Intro Embedded 20260630
chunks.content:
## Conclusion

- An embedded system contains tiny computers to control and decide.
- The embedded computer usually takes the form of a microcontroller:
  - Microprocessor core
  - Memory
  - Peripherals
- Embedded system design combines both hardware and software design.
- The microcontroller has an instruction set used for programming.
- Most programming is done in high level language, then compiled into binary recognized by the microcontroller.
- ARM has developed a range of effective microprocessor and microcontroller designs, widely applied in embedded systems.

# Microcontroller Lec02–03 Supplement — STM32F7 Reference (2026)

> 📄 [View original PDF](documents/microcontroller-lec02-03-supplement-20260630.pdf) — source of truth

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 03 Supplement 20260630
chunks.content:
## STM32F7 Block Diagram

> 📄 The block diagram is a visual diagram and cannot be fully represented in text. See [PDF page 1](documents/microcontroller-lec02-03-supplement-20260630.pdf#page=1) — Figure 2: STM32F765xx, STM32F767xx, STM32F768Ax and STM32F769xx block diagram.

**Key components visible in the diagram include:**

- **ARM Cortex-M7 CPU** with FPU, MPU, NVIC, ETM, I-Cache (16 KB) and D-Cache (16 KB)
- **AHB Bus-Matrix** (11S8M and 8S7M)
- **Memory:** Flash 1 MB, SRAM1 368 KB, SRAM2 16 KB, DTCM RAM 128 KB, ITCM RAM 16 KB, 4 KB Backup RAM
- **GPIO Ports:** A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K (PA[15:0] through PK[7:0])
- **Timers:** TIM1–TIM14, LPTIM1
- **Communication:** USART1/2/3/6, UART4/5/7/8, SPI1–6/I2S1–3, I2C1–4/SMBus, bxCAN1–3, SDMMC1/2, Ethernet MAC, USB OTG HS/FS, Quad-SPI, SAI1/2, SPDIFRX, HDMI-CEC
- **Analog:** 3× ADC (12-bit), 2× DAC (12-bit), temperature sensor
- **Display:** LCD-TFT controller, Chrom-ART (DMA2D), DSI Host with PHY
- **Other:** JPEG codec, DFSDM, RNG, CRC, RTC, WWDG, External Memory Controller (FMC), DMA1/2

> Note: The timers connected to APB2 are clocked from TIMxCLK up to 216 MHz, while timers connected to APB1 are clocked from TIMxCLK up to 108 MHz or 216 MHz depending on the TIMPRE bit in RCC_DCKCFGR.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 03 Supplement 20260630
chunks.content:
## Memory Map

> 📄 See [PDF page 2](documents/microcontroller-lec02-03-supplement-20260630.pdf#page=2) — Figure 2: Memory map.

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 03 Supplement 20260630
chunks.content:
| Block | Address Range | Contents |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| **Block 0** | `0x0000 0000` – `0x1FFF FFFF` | Flash, ITCM RAM, System memory, Option bytes |
| — Flash (AXIM) | `0x0800 0000` – `0x081F FFFF` | 2 MB Flash memory on AXIM interface |
| — Flash (ITCM) | `0x0020 0000` – `0x003F FFFF` | Flash memory on ITCM interface |
| — ITCM RAM | `0x0000 0000` – `0x0000 3FFF` | 16 KB ITCM RAM |
| — System memory | `0x0010 0000` – `0x0010 EDBF` | Bootloader |
| — Option bytes | `0x1FFF 0000` – `0x1FFF 001F` | Configuration |
| **Block 1** | `0x2000 0000` – `0x3FFF FFFF` | SRAM |
| — SRAM1 | `0x2000 0000` – `0x2001 FFFF` | 368 KB |
| — SRAM2 | `0x2007 C000` – `0x2007 FFFF` | 16 KB |
| **Block 2** | `0x4000 0000` – `0x5FFF FFFF` | Peripherals |
| — APB1 | `0x4000 0000` – `0x4000 7FFF` | APB1 peripherals |
| — APB2 | `0x4001 0000` – `0x4001 6BFF` | APB2 peripherals |
| — AHB1 | `0x4002 0000` – `0x4007 FFFF` | AHB1 peripherals |
| — AHB2 | `0x5000 0000` – `0x5006 0BFF` | AHB2 peripherals |
| — AHB3 | `0x6000 0000` – `0xDFFF FFFF` | FMC (external memory) |
| **Block 3** | `0x6000 0000` – `0x7FFF FFFF` | FMC bank 1–2 |
| **Block 4** | `0x8000 0000` – `0x9FFF FFFF` | Quad-SPI and FMC bank 3 |
| **Block 5** | `0xC000 0000` – `0xCFFF FFFF` | FMC |
| **Block 6** | `0xD000 0000` – `0xDFFF FFFF` | FMC |
| **Block 7** | `0xE000 0000` – `0xFFFF FFFF` | Cortex-M7 internal peripherals (NVIC, SysTick, etc.) |
| — DTCM | `0x2000 0000` | DTCM RAM (128 KB) |

## ARM Cortex Architecture

- **Harvard architecture** — separate buses for instructions and data

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
### Cortex Family Comparison

| Feature | Cortex-A8 | Cortex-R4 | Cortex-M3 |
|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Architecture | ARMv7-A | ARMv7-R | ARMv7-M |
| MMU / MPU | MMU | MPU (optional) | MPU (optional) |
| Bus | AXI | AXI | AHB Lite & APB |
| Extras | VFP & NEON | Dual Issue | — |

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Cortex-M0

- Ultra low gate count (less than 12K gates)
- Ultra low-power (3 μW/MHz)
- 32-bit processor
- Based on ARMv6-M architecture

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Cortex-M3

- The mainstream ARM processor for microcontroller applications
- High performance and energy efficiency
- Easy migration path from FPGA to ASIC
- Advanced 3-stage pipeline
- Based on ARMv7-M architecture

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Cortex-M4

- Embedded processor for DSP
- FPU (Floating Point Unit)
- Based on ARMv7E-M architecture

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
#### Cortex-M7

- Highest performance Cortex-M processor
- Superscalar 6-stage pipeline
- Optional double-precision FPU
- Based on ARMv7E-M architecture

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
### 1. RAM (Random Access Memory)

- **Volatile**
- Static RAM (MOS/MOSFET)
- Dynamic RAM (Capacitor)
- Used for: local variables, stack, heap

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
### 2. ROM (Read Only Memory)

- **Non-volatile**
- More complicated to write
- Used for: code + constants

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
## Types of ROM

| Type | Full Name | Description |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Masked ROM | — | Programmed at manufacturing; cannot be changed |
| PROM | Programmable Read-Only Memory | One-time programmable by user |
| EPROM | Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory | Erased by UV light, then reprogrammed |
| EEPROM | Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory | Erased and programmed electrically, one byte at a time |
| Flash Memory | — | Erased and programmed in blocks; NOR / NAND variants |

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
## EEPROM vs Flash Memory

> 📄 See [PDF page 33](documents/microcontroller-lec02-cortex-arch-20260630.pdf#page=33) for comparison diagram.

| Characteristic | EEPROM | Flash Memory |
|---------------|--------|-------------|
| Erase granularity | One byte at a time | In blocks |
| Write speed | Slower (~10 s per byte) | Faster (~1 s per byte) |
| Erase/write cycles | ~10,000–100,000 | Unlimited |
| Cost | More expensive | Less expensive |
| Typical use | External memory for microcontroller | Store code inside microcontroller |

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec02 Cortex Arch 20260630
chunks.content:
## Summary

- Simple register made from D Flip-Flops — stores digital bits
- Cortex-M is **Harvard architecture** (separate instruction and data buses)
- Cortex-M has **16 registers**:
  - 13 general-purpose registers
  - 3 special registers: SP (Stack Pointer), LR (Link Register), PC (Program Counter)
- **N, Z, C, V** flags of the Program Status Register
- **Memory types:**
  - RAM (volatile — SRAM, DRAM)
  - ROM (non-volatile — Masked ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash)

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## Working with Larger DC Loads

- MCU can drive simple DC loads directly with its digital I/O pins (±25 mA)
- MCU can't drive a load (e.g. motor) which needs more current than the MCU port pin can supply
- Need an interfacing circuit to draw current from a higher voltage

- A good switching transistor for small DC loads is the **ZVN4206A**
- The maximum VGS threshold value is 3 V
- MOSFET will respond to the 3.3V Logic 1 output level of the MCU

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## Memory Organization

- Program memory, data memory, registers, and I/O ports are organized within the same linear **4-Gbyte address space**
- Bytes are coded in memory in **little endian** format
  - The lowest numbered byte in a word is the word's least significant byte; the highest numbered byte is the most significant
- Addressable memory space is divided into **8 main blocks**, each of 512 MB

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## GPIO Registers

Each general-purpose I/O port has:

- Four 32-bit configuration registers: `GPIOx_MODER`, `GPIOx_OTYPER`, `GPIOx_OSPEEDR`, `GPIOx_PUPDR`
- Two 32-bit data registers: `GPIOx_IDR`, `GPIOx_ODR`
- 32-bit set/reset register: `GPIOx_BSRR`
- 32-bit locking register: `GPIOx_LCKR`
- Two 32-bit alternate function registers: `GPIOx_AFRH`, `GPIOx_AFRL`

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
### GPIO Pin Modes

Each port bit can be individually configured in software:

| Mode | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Input floating | No pull-up/pull-down |
| Input pull-up | Weak pull-up enabled |
| Input pull-down | Weak pull-down enabled |
| Analog | ADC/DAC connection |
| Output open-drain | With pull-up/pull-down capability |
| Output push-pull | With pull-up/pull-down capability |
| Alternate function push-pull | Peripheral-driven, push-pull |
| Alternate function open-drain | Peripheral-driven, open-drain |

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630

chunks.content:
### Basic Structure of a GPIO Bit (5V Tolerant)

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## GPIO Operation

- During and just after reset, alternate functions are **not active** and I/O ports are configured in **Input Floating mode** (`MODERx[1:0]=00b`, `PUPDRx[1:0]=00b`)
- When configured as output, the value written to `GPIOx_ODR` is output on the I/O pin
  - Push-Pull mode or Open-Drain mode (only N-MOS activated when outputting 0)
- `GPIOx_IDR` captures the data present on the I/O pin at every AHB1 clock cycle
- All GPIO pins have internal weak pull-up and weak pull-down which can be activated when configured as input

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.section: GPIO Atomic Bit Set or Reset
chunks.content:
## GPIO Atomic Bit Set or Reset

- **Atomic Read/Modify access** — no interruption in the middle to cause errors
- Atomic operations ensure the desired change is not interrupted (no partial set/reset)
- No need to disable interrupts when programming `GPIOx_ODR` at bit level
- Modify only one or several bits in a single atomic AHB1 write access
- Program `1` to the Bit Set/Reset Register (`GPIOx_BSRR`, or `GPIOx_BRR` for reset only) to select bits to modify
  - Unselected bits are not modified

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## Input Configuration

When the I/O Port is programmed as **Input**:

- The Output Buffer is **disabled**
- The Schmitt Trigger Input is **activated**
- Weak pull-up and pull-down resistors are activated or not depending on configuration (pull-up, pull-down, or floating)
- Data present on the I/O pin is sampled into the Input Data Register every AHB1 clock cycle
- A read access to the Input Data Register obtains the I/O state

> 📄 See [PDF page 27](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=27) — input configuration diagram.

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
### Schmitt Trigger

> 📄 See [PDF page 28–29](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=28) — Schmitt trigger circuit and output waveform.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## Output Configuration

When the I/O Port is programmed as **Output**:

- The Output Buffer is **enabled**:
  - **Open Drain Mode:** A `0` in the Output register activates the N-MOS; a `1` leaves the port in Hi-Z (P-MOS never activated)
  - **Push-Pull Mode:** A `0` activates the N-MOS; a `1` activates the P-MOS
- The Schmitt Trigger Input is **activated**
- Weak pull-up and pull-down resistors are **disabled**
- Data present on the I/O pin is sampled into `GPIOx_IDR` every AHB1 clock cycle
- Read `GPIOx_IDR` gets the I/O state; read `GPIOx_ODR` gets the last written value

> 📄 See [PDF page 31–33](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=31) — output configuration, data register, and push-pull diagrams.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## Alternate Function Configuration

When the I/O Port is programmed as **Alternate Function**:

- The Output Buffer is turned on in Open Drain or Push-Pull configuration
- The Output Buffer is driven by the signal from the peripheral (alternate function out)
- The Schmitt Trigger Input is **activated**
- Weak pull-up and pull-down resistors are **disabled**
- Data present on the I/O pin is sampled into `GPIOx_IDR` every AHB1 clock cycle
- Read `GPIOx_IDR` gets the I/O state (open drain mode)
- Read `GPIOx_ODR` gets the last written value (push-pull mode)

> 📄 See [PDF page 35–36](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=35) — alternate function configuration diagrams.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## Analog Configuration

When the I/O Port is programmed as **Analog**:

- The Output Buffer is **disabled**
- The Schmitt Trigger Input is **de-activated** — zero consumption for every analog value
- The output of the Schmitt Trigger is forced to a constant value (0)
- Weak pull-up and pull-down resistors are **disabled**
- Read access to the Input Data Register gets the value `0`

> 📄 See [PDF page 38](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=38) — analog I/O configuration diagram.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec03 Gpio 20260630
chunks.content:
## GPIO Register Reference

> 📄 Register bit layouts — see [PDF pages 39–50](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=39)

| Register | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `GPIOx_MODER` | Port mode register |
| `GPIOx_OTYPER` | Port output type register |
| `GPIOx_OSPEEDR` | Port output speed register |
| `GPIOx_PUPDR` | Port pull-up/pull-down register |
| `GPIOx_IDR` | Port input data register |
| `GPIOx_ODR` | Port output data register |
| `GPIOx_BSRR` | Port bit set/reset register |
| `GPIOx_LCKR` | Port configuration lock register |
| `GPIOx_AFRL` | Alternate function low register |
| `GPIOx_AFRH` | Alternate function high register |

> 📄 GPIO Bit Configuration Table — see [PDF page 50–51](documents/microcontroller-lec03-gpio-20260630.pdf#page=50)

---

```

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## C Compiler Data Types

Always match data type to data characteristics:
- `#bits` determines range of numeric values
- `signed/unsigned` determines which arithmetic/relational operators are used
- Non-numeric data should be **unsigned**
- Header file `stdint.h` defines alternate type names — eliminates ambiguity

| Declaration | Bits | Range |
|-------------|------|-------|
| `char k;` / `unsigned char k;` / `uint8_t k;` | 8 | 0 … 255 |
| `signed char k;` / `int8_t k;` | 8 | −128 … +127 |
| `short k;` / `signed short k;` / `int16_t k;` | 16 | −32,768 … +32,767 |
| `unsigned short k;` / `uint16_t k;` | 16 | 0 … 65,535 |
| `int k;` / `signed int k;` / `int32_t k;` | 32 | −2,147,483,648 … +2,147,483,647 |
| `unsigned int k;` / `uint32_t k;` | 32 | 0 … 4,294,967,295 |

> `intx_t` and `uintx_t` defined in `stdint.h`

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Data Type Examples

```c
uint16_t n;   n = GPIOA->IDR;    // Read bits from GPIOA (16 bits, non-numeric)
uint16_t t;   TIM2->PSC = t;     // Write TIM2 prescale value (16-bit unsigned)
uint32_t a;   a = ADC;           // Read 32-bit value from ADC (unsigned)
int32_t ctrl; ctrl = (x + y)*z;  // System control value range [-1000…+1000]
uint8_t cnt;                     // Loop counter for 100 program loops (unsigned)
for (cnt = 0; cnt < 20; cnt++) { … }
```

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Constant / Literal Values

- **Decimal** is the default format: `m = 453; n = -25;`
- **Hexadecimal:** preface with `0x` or `0X`: `m = 0xF312; n = -0x12E4;`
- **Octal:** preface with zero: `m = 0453; n = -023;`
  - ⚠️ Don't use leading zeros on decimal values — they'll be interpreted as octal!
- **Character:** single quotes or ASCII escape: `m = 'a';` (0x61), `n = '\13';` (return)
- **String (array):** `strcpy(m, "hello\n");`

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Program Variables

A variable is an addressable storage location. Each must be declared with size, type, and name.

```c
int x, y, z;    // declares 3 variables of type "int"
char a, b;      // declares 2 variables of type "char"
```

- Space may be allocated in registers, RAM, or ROM/Flash (for constants)
- Variables can be **automatic** or **static**

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Variable Arrays

An array is a set of data stored in consecutive memory locations.

```c
int n[5];       // declare array of 5 "int" values
n[3] = 5;       // set value of 4th array element
```

```
Address:  n      n+4    n+8    n+12   n+16
          n[0]   n[1]   n[2]   n[3]   n[4]
```

> Note: Index of first element is always 0.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## C Statement Types

- Simple variable assignments (includes input/output data transfers)
- Arithmetic operations
- Logical/shift operations
- Control structures: `if`, `while`, `for`, `switch`
- Function calls (user-defined and/or library functions)

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Arithmetic Operations

```c
int i, j, k;       // 32-bit signed integers
uint8_t m, n, p;   // 8-bit unsigned numbers

i = j + k;          // add 32-bit integers
m = n - 5;          // subtract 8-bit numbers
j = i * k;          // multiply 32-bit integers
m = n / p;          // quotient of 8-bit divide
m = n % p;          // remainder of 8-bit divide
i = (j + k) * (i - 2);  // arithmetic expression
```

> `*`, `/`, `%` have higher precedence than `+`, `-`.  
> Example: `j * k + m / n` = `(j * k) + (m / n)`  
> ⚠️ Floating-point formats are **not directly supported** by Cortex-M3 CPUs.

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Bit-Parallel Logical Operators

| Operator | Name | Example |
|----------|------|---------|
| `&` | AND | `C = A & B;` |
| `\|` | OR | `C = A \| B;` |
| `^` | XOR | `C = A ^ B;` |
| `~` | Complement (NOT) | `B = ~A;` |

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Bit Set / Reset / Complement / Test

Use a **mask** to select bit(s) to be altered:

| Operation | Expression | Effect |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Clear bit 0 | `C = A & 0xFE;` | Clears LSB: `abcdefg0` |
| Isolate bit 0 | `C = A & 0x01;` | Mask all but LSB: `0000000h` |
| Set bit 0 | `C = A \| 0x01;` | Sets LSB: `abcdefg1` |
| Toggle bit 0 | `C = A ^ 0x01;` | Complements LSB: `abcdefgh'` |

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.section: Create a Pulse on Bit 0 of PORTA
chunks.content:
### Create a Pulse on Bit 0 of PORTA

```c
// Assume bit is initially 0
PORTA = PORTA | 0x01;     // Force bit 0 to 1
PORTA = PORTA & 0xFE;     // Force bit 0 to 0
```

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Test and Set Bits

```c
if ((PORTA & 0x80) != 0)    // call bob() if bit 7 of PORTA is 1
    bob();
c = PORTB & 0x04;            // mask all but bit 2 of PORTB value
if ((PORTA & 0x01) == 0)    // test bit 0 of PORTA
    PORTA = c | 0x01;        // write c to PORTA with bit 0 set to 1
```

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Using GPIO Register Pointers (STM32)

```c
#define GPIO_PIN_0   ((uint16_t) 0x0001U)

GPIOA->ODR = GPIOA->ODR | 0x01UL;        // Force bit 0 to 1
GPIOA->ODR = GPIOA->ODR & 0xFEUL;        // Force bit 0 to 0
GPIOA->ODR = GPIOA->ODR & ~(0x01UL);     // Force bit 0 to 0 (alternate)

if ((GPIOA->IDR & 0x80) != 0UL)          // call bob() if bit 7 of GPIOA is 1
    bob();
c = GPIOB->IDR & 0x04UL;                 // mask all but bit 2 of GPIOB
if ((GPIOA->IDR & 0x01UL) == 0UL)
    GPIOA->ODR = c | 0x01;               // write c to PORTA with bit 0 set to 1
```

> MISRA C is a set of software development guidelines for the C programming language developed by MISRA (Motor Industry Software Reliability Association).

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Register Address Definitions (stm32f767xx.h)

```c
#define PERIPH_BASE        ((uint32_t) 0x40000000UL)
#define AHB1PERIPH_BASE    (PERIPH_BASE + 0x00020000UL)

/* Base addresses of GPIO control/data register blocks */
#define GPIOA_BASE   (AHB1PERIPH_BASE + 0x0000UL)
#define GPIOB_BASE   (AHB1PERIPH_BASE + 0x0400UL)
#define GPIOA        ((GPIO_TypeDef *) GPIOA_BASE)
#define GPIOB        ((GPIO_TypeDef *) GPIOB_BASE)

/* GPIO register block defined as a structure */
typedef struct {
    __IO uint32_t MODER;     // 0x00 — Port mode register
    __IO uint16_t OTYPER;    // 0x04 — Port output type register
    __IO uint32_t OSPEEDR;   // 0x08 — Port output speed register
    __IO uint32_t PUPDR;     // 0x0C — Port pull-up/pull-down register
    __IO uint32_t IDR;       // 0x10 — Port input data register
    __IO uint32_t ODR;       // 0x14 — Port output data register
    __IO uint32_t BSRR;      // 0x18 — Port bit set/reset register
    __IO uint32_t LCKR;      // 0x1C — Port configuration lock register
    __IO uint32_t AFR[2];    // 0x20-0x24 — Alternate function registers
} GPIO_TypeDef;
```

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Example: I/O Port Bit Operations

```c
uint32_t sw;
sw = GPIOB->IDR;               // sw = xxxxxxxxhgfedcba (upper 8 bits from PB15-PB8)

sw = GPIOB->IDR & 0x0010;      // sw = 000e0000 (mask all but bit 4)

// Common errors and correct forms:
if (sw == 0x01)   {}           // NEVER TRUE — sw is 000e0000, not 00000001
if (sw == 0x10)   {}           // TRUE if e=1 (bit 4 in result of PORTB & 0x10)
if (sw == 0)      {}           // TRUE if e=0 (switch not pressed)
if (sw != 0)      {}           // TRUE if e=1 (switch pressed)

GPIOB->ODR = 0x005a;           // Write to 16 bits of GPIOB: 01011010
GPIOB->ODR |= 0x10;            // Set only bit 4 to 1
GPIOB->ODR &= ~0x10;           // Reset only bit 4 to 0
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "GPIOB bits"
        direction LR
        h["h"] --- g["g"] --- f["f"] --- e["e"] --- d["d"] --- c["c"] --- b["b"] --- a["a"]
    end
    Switch["Switch"] --> e
```

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Shift Operators

| Operator | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| `x << y` | Left shift operand x by y bit positions |
| `x >> y` | Right shift operand x by y bit positions |

Vacated bits are filled with 0's. Shift is a fast way to multiply/divide by powers of 2.

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Left Shift Example

```
B = A << 3;
A:  1 0 1 0 1 1 0 1
B:  0 1 1 0 1 0 0 0   (left shift 3 bits)
```

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Right Shift Example

```
B = A >> 2;
A:  1 0 1 1 0 1 0 1
B:  0 0 1 0 1 1 0 1   (right shift 2 bits)
```

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Control Structures

Control the order in which instructions are executed (program flow):

- **Conditional execution** — execute statements if condition met; select from options
- **Iterative execution** — repeat statements a specified number of times, until condition met, or while condition is true

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Relational Operators

| Test | TRUE condition | Notes |
|------|---------------|-------|
| `(m == b)` | m equal to b | Double `=` |
| `(m != b)` | m not equal to b | |
| `(m < b)` | m less than b | signed/unsigned per data type |
| `(m <= b)` | m less than or equal to b | |
| `(m > b)` | m greater than b | |
| `(m >= b)` | m greater than or equal to b | |
| `(m)` | m non-zero | |
| `(1)` | always TRUE | |
| `(0)` | always FALSE | |

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Boolean Operators

Boolean operators `&&` (AND) and `||` (OR) produce TRUE/FALSE results for multiple conditions.

```c
if ((n > 1) && (n < 5))          // test for n between 1 and 5
if ((c == 'q') || (c == 'Q'))    // test c = lower or upper case Q
```

> ⚠️ Boolean `&&`, `||` vs bitwise `&`, `|`:  
> `if (k && m)` — test if k and m both TRUE (non-zero)  
> `if (k & m)` — compute bitwise AND, then test if result is non-zero

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Common Error

`==` is a relational operator; `=` is an assignment operator.

```c
if (m == n)   // tests equality of m and n  ← CORRECT
if (m = n)    // assigns n to m, then tests if result is TRUE ← ERROR
```

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## SWITCH Statement

Compact alternative to ELSE-IF for multi-way decisions testing one variable against constant values.

```c
switch (n) {
    case 0:  statement1; break;   // do if n == 0
    case 1:  statement2; break;   // do if n == 1
    case 2:  statement3; break;   // do if n == 2
    default: statement4; break;    // any other value
}
```

---

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## C Functions

Functions partition large programs into smaller tasks:
- Helps manage program complexity
- Smaller tasks are easier to design and debug
- Functions can be reused
- Can use "libraries" of functions developed by 3rd parties

A function is **called** by another program to perform a task:
- May return a result to the caller
- One or more arguments may be passed to the function

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Function Definition

```c
int math_func(int k, int n) {   // parameters passed by caller
    int j;                       // local variable
    j = n + k - 5;              // function body
    return(j);                   // return the result
}
// Type of value returned to caller — if none, specify "void"
```

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
### Function Arguments

- **By value:** pass a constant or variable value — function can use but not modify
- **By reference:** pass the address of the variable — function can read and update
- Values/addresses are typically passed by pushing them onto the system stack

chunks.title: Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
Microcontroller Lec04 Embedded C 20260630
chunks.content:
## Finite State Machine

> 📄 See [PDF final pages](documents/microcontroller-lec04-embedded-c-20260630.pdf) — Mealy and Moore FSM model diagrams.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
# CEI Orientation — Year 3 & Year 4 Roadmap

> Organised from voice recording — July 2026 orientation.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## Overview of Required & Elective Components

| Component | When | Credits | Grade | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CEPP** | Y3S2 | 1 | A/B/C | 1 semester |
| **Internship** | After Y3S2 (break) | 0 | S/U | ≥2 months |
| **Cooperative Study** | Y4S1 | 6 | A/B/C | ≥4 months |
| **Capstone Project 1** | Y4S1 | 3 | A/B/C | 1 semester |
| **Capstone Project 2** | Y4S2 | 3 | A/B/C | 1 semester |
| **Oversee Training** | Y4S1 | 6 | A/B/C | 1 semester |
| **Study Abroad** | Y4S1 | 6 | A/B/C | 1 semester |

- **CEPP and Internship are mandatory for everyone.**
- For Y4S1 you must pick **one** of: Cooperative Study, Capstone Project, Oversee Training, or Study Abroad.
- Coop and Capstone are the two main tracks; you do **either** coop **or** capstone (not both).

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 1. CEPP (Computer Engineering Preliminary Project)

**When:** Y3S2, but you can start preparing now (July).

- Group of **3** (can be more).
- Choose a topic, then find an advisor.
- Meet with advisor weekly or regularly.
- Submit a proposal/report at the end of the semester.
- At the end, give a presentation and receive comments.
- **1 credit** (according to orientation, 2 credits in the curriculum document), graded A/B/C.
- Think of it as a "mini capstone" — a project simulation.
- A Google Classroom for CEPP will be announced soon.

> If someone in your group later decides to go for Coop instead of Capstone, that is fine — groups can be rearranged later.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 2. Internship

**When:** After Y3S2 (summer break, not overlapping with a regular semester).  
**Duration:** Minimum **8 weeks (2 months)**.  
**Credits:** 0 (required but no credits), graded **S/U**.  
**Registration:** Opens in April. No tuition fee.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Deadlines

- Submit internship report and diary by **15 July** (15 days to prepare after finishing).
- All paperwork must be submitted before going.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.section: Section IDs (register in the correct group!)
chunks.content:
### Section IDs (register in the correct group!)

| Section ID | Who |
|---|---|
| 901 | 2nd & 3rd year students |
| 902 | 4th year students expected to graduate |
| 903 | International internship / exchange abroad |

> **Registering in the wrong group = Grade I (Incomplete).**

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Documents — Internship

1. Fill **SI101** and **SI201**.
2. Combine with: student ID, resume, transcript.
3. Send to **Dr. Kanut** for signature.
4. Then send to **P'Ploy** via email.

Student Affairs will issue an **acceptance form**. You must:
- Ask the company to fill it in.
- Return it to Student Affairs.
- Student Affairs will then send the official internship placement letter to the company.
- *You may need to juggle forwarding documents between parties.*

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### During Internship

- Keep a **diary** with daily time-in / time-out.
- Report weekly or regularly on what you did.
- Maximum **3 days sick and personal leave** (requires medical certificates submitted with the diary).
- 3 late arrivals = 1 absence.
- Do **not** take company secrets outside — write something generic in the diary if needed.
- Work schedule depends on the company (6-day work week, holidays, etc.).

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Internship + Coop at Same Company

If you do both at the same company:
- Internship: 2 months (SI documents).
- Coop: 4 months (COOP documents).
- **Keep the documents completely separate.**
- Total = 6 months.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 3. Cooperative Study (Coop)

**When:** Y4S1  
**Duration:** 4 months (full semester).  
**Credits:** 6 (equivalent to Capstone 1 + 2).  
**Grade:** A/B/C.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Constraints

- If you register for Coop, Oversee Training, or Study Abroad, you **cannot** register for any other courses.
- No leave exceeding **10%** of total work time.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Documents — Coop

Before going:
1. Find a company and get their acceptance.
2. Fill **COOP 101, 102, 103**.
3. Send to **Prof. Kanut** to sign.
4. Combine with transcript and student ID.
5. Send to **P'Ploy**.

An acceptance form will be issued; you must:
- Have the company sign and return it to you.
- Return it to the university.

During and after Coop:
- During: submit **COOP 104, 105**.
- After: submit a **report** and **poster** (a format/template will be provided).
- The CE department may require you to **present** the project you did with the company.
- There will be a **university visit** at the company.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Timeline

- Around July, P'Ploy will combine documents and send them to the Line group.
- Some files come from Student Affairs after you submit COOP 101/102.
- Grades are announced around **December or January** after assessment.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 4. Capstone Project

**When:** Y4S1 (Capstone 1) and Y4S2 (Capstone 2).  
**Credits:** 3 + 3 = 6 total.  
**Grade:** A/B/C.

- You can **continue** your CEPP plan into Capstone 1.
- **Capstone 1:** Submit report and do presentations at the end of the semester.
- **Capstone 2:** Harder than Capstone 1; includes a **demonstration** and a **final report**.
- If doing capstone with a professor, you **can** also register for GenEd courses.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
### Oversee Training

- Credits can be transferred to: internship, free GenEd, possibly Coop.
- Can be done in Thailand or abroad.

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 6. Supervisor Requirement

In Y4, **regardless of which track you choose** (Capstone, Coop, Oversee Training, or Study Abroad), you will need a **supervisor** to assess your grade.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 7. Finding a Company

- Start looking from **Y3S1** (now!).
- If you don't do the internship in the planned time, it will affect your **honours**.

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 8. Scholarships

| Scholarship | Amount | Where to Ask |
|---|---|---|
| OIA | 40,000 THB | [oia.kmitl.ac.th](https://oia.kmitl.ac.th/scholarship-students/#) |
| School of Engineering | 30,000 THB | [facebook](https://www.facebook.com/kmitl.engineer.inter) |
| CEI | 20,000 THB | Ask P'Ploy |

---

chunks.title: Orientation Y3 20260706
chunks.content:
## 9. Important Reminders

- For internships you find yourself, you may apply to multiple places.
- Internships/Overseas Training obtained through a contactor (e.g. **Prof. Rathachai**) are **not cancelable** — do not apply elsewhere if you take one of these.
- All options (internship, coop, capstone, oversee training, study abroad) can be done in Thailand or abroad.

chunks.title: Os Cs162 Reference 20260719
chunks.content:
### Processes & Protection (Lectures 1–5)
OS fundamentals: what an OS is, protection domains, processes, I/O,
files, and threading primitives.

chunks.title: Os Cs162 Reference 20260719
chunks.content:
### Synchronization (Lectures 6–9)
Concurrency, mutual exclusion, atomic instructions, lock implementation,
semaphores, monitors, and the readers/writers problem.

chunks.title: Os Cs162 Reference 20260719
chunks.content:
### Scheduling & Deadlock (Lectures 10–13)
Scheduling policies, core concepts, and deadlock detection and prevention.

chunks.title: Os Cs162 Reference 20260719
chunks.content:
### Virtual Memory (Lectures 14–16)
Address spaces, paging, page tables, TLBs, and demand paging.

chunks.title: Os Cs162 Reference 20260719
chunks.content:
### I/O & File Systems (Lectures 17–20)
General I/O abstractions, disk scheduling, file system design,
and reliability.

chunks.title: Os Cs162 Reference 20260719
chunks.content:
### Distributed Systems (Lectures 21–24)
Distributed file systems, networking, data processing, coordination,
and atomic commit protocols.
